- RICH | The Ultimate Guide to Rich People Investment | Investing Strategies of TOP 1% | IN HINDI: Kind: captions Language: en Today we're going to discuss a topic that's often surrounded by speculation: the assets that make rich people even richer. Have you ever wondered how rich people increase their wealth? Hello, everyone, welcome to The Optimist Indian. In this video, we'll uncover the 15 assets that ultra- wealthy people invest in to secure and expand their wealth. From real estate to the stock market, this asset is n't just for the elite class. You can also be a part of it. We'll break down each asset and explain how you can start investing in it. Whether you're a budding investor or just want to learn the secrets of the rich, this video is packed with valuable insights. So, relax and let's explore the world of high-return investments. Number One Mutual Funds and Index Funds. While stocks allow you to purchase shares of a company individually, mutual funds and index funds bring together multiple companies, meaning you invest in a whole bunch of companies. Why is it better? Because it diversifies you more, making investments safer. Statistically, index funds are one of the best-performing asset classes. If you start buying stocks one by one, statically, you're more likely to make mistakes. You're not one of those miracle traders who beat the market year after year. This is where index funds come in. They bring together the Nifty 50, and when a company falls from that top 50, a new, better-performing company replaces it. Over the past 30 years, the Nifty 50 index has had an average annual return of over 12%. Index funds should be in every portfolio of those who want to build long-term wealth. Number two: Bonds. When governments or businesses need cash, they issue bonds that are sold to interested investors. Through these bonds, the government or business promises to pay a certain amount of money every month to the person buying the bonds. Generally, bonds have a fixed price. Expiry dates range from one month to 30 years. These are a super safe investment because they're mostly managed by the government. But why bonds aren't good for a new investor? As you may know, low risk in business means low returns. Bonds typically offer a 3% annual return, which is lower than most banks, but it's not a high enough return to excite a beginner investor. When the bonds reach their expiry date, the investor gets their principal back. People can buy bonds in two main ways: directly from the Treasury Department and from a brokerage firm. Number three, real estate, real estate. I personally like them because they offer two advantages: first, monthly rent payments, and a minimum amount of work. If you're smart, you can take advantage of current technology improvements and focus on short-term rentals instead of traditional rentals. This can offer higher returns, but it's not as passive. Number two is appreciation. Population numbers are rising, and more and more people need rented space to live. Demand is increasing, which is why property prices are constantly rising. With ever-increasing demand, real estate can be a better investment plan. Now, let's look at real estate terms, which are of four types: residential buildings, office buildings, commercial buildings, and land. Residential buildings where people live in your properties and pay you rent. Office buildings where people invest in your properties. [Music] Now, stocks aren't as complicated as you might believe. Stocks are a simple way to own a percentage of a publicly traded business. So, if a business issues 1 million shares and you own 100,000 of those shares, you own 10% of that business. This incredible breakthrough in financial instruments has allowed the average person to own stakes in some of the most lucrative companies of our time, and with very low barriers. If you liked this video, do n't forget to subscribe to the channel, Number Five Cash. Cash deposited in the bank earns you interest. It's quite straightforward. The downside is that the interest rates banks are paying these days are n't keeping pace with inflation, but cash has also become a priority. Imagine your dream deal is right in front of you and you don't have the money to take it. This could be very bad. This is the main reason why wealthy people save cash, and secondly, because they can get higher returns from straight cash deals. People offer better pricing for cash can also lend their money at higher interest rates. This practice is called peer-to-peer lending. If your income is low and you're willing to take this risk, there are excellent platforms that offer peer-to-peer lending services. While the average bank pays you interest at 6%, peer-to-peer lending fluctuates between 7% and 15%. Number six: equipment, anything that helps you earn money is an asset. Consider: If you're a farmer, a tractor is an asset. If you're a programmer, a laptop is an asset. If you're a driver, a car is an asset. Whether something is an asset or a liability depends on whether it's directly related to your income generation. So, unless your income directly depends on the car, buying a new car is a liability. Many times, people confuse the two, and you mismanage your finances, suffer from a lack of self-control, and try to justify the car as an asset. youtube1 Patent: When you invent something new, you can file a patent to protect your invention. This is a document that certifies you as an inventor and explains in detail what your invention does. With this protection, companies can pay you to use an invention, and if they don't, you can sue it. Patents are very powerful in the business world. A single patent can make you rich, and the best inventions are backed by patents. Number eight: Trademarks protect your invention, while patents protect symbols, words, or phrases. This is obvious, because brands have important logos or names. But these things get more interesting. If you have a valuable trademark that has market value, you can license it and offer it to people for commercial purposes at a high return. The trademark can be local or international. Number nine: Brand and goodwill: There is a difference between a brand and goodwill, but both are extremely valuable to a business. A brand is the footprint a company leaves in the minds of its consumers, and goodwill is the emotional affection people have for the brand. Brand ownership is a company's efforts to promote a particular image in the marketplace. Goodwill is the way a company treats its customers. From the way things are done to the positive impact on the community and the gratitude people feel that the company has. Goodwill is super hard to cultivate or scale, but people literally save companies from bankers' money because they have goodwill. Number 10: People. You don't realize how valuable assets people are until they leave. Very few people understand that companies are not actually real. Companies are part of an imagination validated by the state. Companies are ideas and innovations that exist under an imaginary umbrella. And who brings all these ideas are the people of the company? A single person can transform a company with their idea. - Germany: The discreet lives of the super rich | DW Documentary: Kind: captions Language: id Jerman super kaya. Tidak ada negara Eropa lain yang memiliki miliarder sebanyak Jerman. Dan sementara kekayaan mereka bertambah, semakin banyak orang Jerman hidup di bawah garis kemiskinan yang ditetapkan Organisasi untuk Kerjasama Ekonomi dan Pembangunan OECD. Pers sering melaporkan tingginya ketidaksetaraan pendapatan negara itu dan mobilitas sosial yang rendah. Tetapi sedikit yang diketahui tentang orang super kaya. Tentu uang menarik sukses dan sukses menarik uang. Saya percaya itu. Saya telah alami sendiri bagaimana Anda tiba-tiba bisa terhubung dengan orang-orang yang sebelumnya tidak terjangkau. Siapakah orang-orang super kaya Jerman? Bagaimana mereka hidup? Dan bagaimana mereka melihat negara tempat mereka tinggal? Cara orang-orang kaya di Jerman dibicarakan mengganggu saya, karena itu sensasional dan memalukan, dan sama sekali tidak mencerminkan apa yang telah dilakukan orang kaya, dan terus dilakukan, untuk negara ini. Ini menciptakan kesan orang kaya seperti Paman Gober Bebek. Bahwa mereka punya gudang uang tempat mereka berendam dalam koin mereka. Paman Gober ingin lebih, lebih, lebih banyak! Itu bukan dunia saya. Saya suka mendapatkan uang, tapi bukan berenang di dalamnya. Kami ingin menilik lebih dekat dunia terpisah orang ultrakaya di Jerman - para pemilik dan pewaris perusahaan bernilai miliaran. Kami ingin mengetahui apa yang memotivasi mereka yang berada di peringkat puncak dunia. PERINGKAT PUNCAK DUNIA Kehidupan Tersendiri Para Super Kaya Film karya Florian Opitz Setiap tahun sebuah acara eksklusif berlangsung di Schlosshotel Kronberg dekat Frankfurt, di mana publik tidak diundang. Itu adalah malam "Hall of Fame" tahunan, untuk majalah bulanan Manager Magazine. Hampir tidak ada acara lain di Jerman yang menarik begitu banyak pemilik bisnis kaya. Para moderator yang terhormat, anggota dewan juri yang baik, hadirin yang terhormat, Selamat datang di Hall of Fame Manager Magazine. Ketika kami pertama kali membentuk Hall of Fame pada tahun 1992, kami ingin mengambil sikap untuk keunggulan dan kewirausahaan tanpa syarat, dan melawan hal yang bersifat pengecut dan rata-rata. Kami memulai nominasi kami hari ini dengan Ralph Dommermuth. Perusahaannya, United Internet, bernilai sekitar 11 miliar dan pada daftar kami untuk orang terkaya di Jerman, ia di peringkat ke-25 dengan kekayaan pribadi sebesar 4,5 miliar Euro. Secara kolektif para tamu pada malam yang elegan ini bernilai miliaran Euro. Inilah wajah kekayaan di Jerman: terutama pria. Dan meskipun kami dekat dengan orang super kaya di sini, dunia mereka entah bagaimana tetap tidak terjangkau. Hamburg, rumah bagi induk perusahaan Manager Magazine, Grup Spiegel. Hampir tidak ada publikasi yang melaporkan tentang orang-orang Jerman yang ultrakaya. Setiap tahun tim majalah itu mengumpulkan informasi tentang orang kaya Jerman dan - menggunakan model Forbes - membuat edisi khusus dengan daftar 1001 orang terkaya Jerman. Ini pekerjaan yang susah payah. Berapa miliarder yang ada di sana sekarang? 180 hingga 190? Tahun lalu 136, betul? Tidak. Tahun lalu ada sekitar 170. 170, oke. Siapa yang ingin masuk dalam daftar 1001 orang Jerman terkaya kami, perlu memiliki sekitar 100 juta. Itu tidak harus berupa uang di bank. Kebanyakan orang memilikinya sebagai aset atau sebagai properti. Tapi itulah stadion yang kami cari untuk berada dalam daftar orang-orang Jerman terkaya kami. Pemimpin redaksi Steffen Klusmann telah berada di sekitar orang Jerman ultrakaya selama bertahun-tahun. Apa yang diperlukan untuk masuk dalam daftar? Saya akan mengatakan, 150 tempat teratas dalam daftar kami akan selalu menjadi milik pemilik perusahaan dan ahli waris. Bahkan jika Anda seorang dokter kepala, Anda harus melihat banyak pasien untuk menjadi miliarder. Manajer juga mengalami kesulitan untuk mencapai setinggi itu. Di sini, di Jerman ada perdebatan tentang apakah kepala perusahaan DAX berpenghasilan terlalu banyak. Tapi jika Anda membandingkannya dengan orang-orang pada posisi serupa di AS, Inggris atau Cina dapatkan, itu sangat kecil. Jadi itu saja tidak akan pernah bisa membuat orang menjadi salah satu yang benar-benar ultrakaya. Membuat daftar orang terkaya, terutama sangat sulit di Jerman. Itu bukan karena jumlah mereka kurang. Dia entah bagaimana berhasil mengumpulkan kerajaan dan kekayaan besar ini hanya dalam beberapa tahun. Kekayaannya diperkirakan sekitar empat miliar. Dia adalah orang Jerman super kaya paling tidak dikenal. Apa dia akan membiarkan kita mewawancarainya di rumah? Mereka lebih suka terbang di bawah radar untuk menghindari rasa iri. Orang super kaya tidak suka membicarakan dirinya sendiri, begitu bisa dikatakan. Ya, Anda biasanya hanya bisa mendapatkan wawancara di rumah dengan mereka yang berada di liga kedua. Di sana Anda akan selalu mendapatkan orang yang menyukai publisitas. Seperti Bapak Maschmeyer misalnya. Mendapat foto bagus dari Bapak Maschmeyer tidak pernah menjadi masalah. Tetapi "uang" yang asli tetap menyembunyikan diri. Orang super kaya mencoba untuk tidak menarik perhatian. Kadang-kadang mereka bahkan mencoba bersembunyi. Beberapa orang tidak ada fotonya dalam daftar kami. Anda tak akan temukan satu gambar pun jika Anda pergi online dan mencari mereka di Google. Belum ada foto Reimann, keluarga terkaya Jerman, untuk beberapa dekade. Banyak yang tetap bersembunyi karena mereka ingin menjalani hidup normal dan berpikir mereka tidak akan bisa melakukan itu jika mereka dikenal sebagai multimiliarder. Selama berbulan-bulan permintaan wawancara kami ditolak, pengambilan gambar film yang disetujui dibatalkan pada menit terakhir. Tidak ada dari orang kaya yang mau berbicara dengan kami tentang uang. Akhirnya, kami beruntung di pusat keuangan Frankfurt. Di sini, di lokasi utama yang terletak di belakang menara-menara bank, terdapat perusahaan manajemen aset Focam. Focam mengelola kekayaan keluarga bisnis Jerman. Pimpinannya, Christian von Bechtolsheim, memberi sejumlah wawasan mengapa orang-orang Jerman yang kaya, sangat pemalu. Banyak orang Jerman yang kaya enggan melangkah keluar ke mata publik karena mereka khawatir mereka dapat dilihat secara negatif. Mereka bertanya pada diri sendiri: ‘apa yang saya peroleh dari menunjukkan diri kepada publik? Itu tidak memberi saya apa pun. Sebaliknya, itu bisa menyebabkan orang gila memperhatikan dan membobol rumah saya atau menculik salah satu anak saya.’ Dan ketakutan itu bukan tanpa alasan. Lalu, ada juga fakta bahwa banyak ahli waris yang mewarisi kekayaan, yang entah bagaimana dinodai oleh atau terkait dengan Jerman NAZI atau Reich Ketiga. Jajaran atas orang kaya Jerman menghindari publisitas seolah-olah itu wabah. Tapi apa yang begitu mereka takutkan? Saya bertanya kepada sejumlah teman saya apakah mereka mau diwawancarai untuk film ini, dan masing-masing mengatakan tidak. Kata mereka, ‘orang lain bisa melakukannya lebih baik dibanding saya, saya tak bisa melakukan dengan benar, saya mungkin akan membuat salah.’ Mereka pikir mereka memiliki lebih banyak kerugian daripada manfaat. Setelah banyak bolak-balik dengan tim persnya, seorang Jerman ultrakaya, setuju untuk bertemu kami. Michael Otto adalah Ketua Dewan Pengawas Grup Otto dan salah satu dari sepuluh orang Jerman terkaya. Kami bertanya kepadanya mengapa orang Jerman enggan memamerkan kekayaan mereka. Banyak yang khawatir hal itu akan menimbulkan kecemburuan. Di Amerika Serikat, prestasi dan kekayaan dilihat secara sangat positif. Tapi di sini mereka membawa sisa rasa pahit: ‘Dari mana kekayaannya berasal atau bagaimana dia memperoleh kekayaannya?’ Itu membuat sejumlah orang takut. Saya pikir banyak orang merasa kekayaan mereka sendiri agak memuakkan. Saya sama sekali tidak. Saya suka itu. Dirk Rossmann besar dalam kondisi miskin. Ibunya mengelola toko obat kecil di Hannover pasca perang. Putranya memiliki rencana yang lebih besar. Tahun 1972, gagasan untuk membuka toko produk kesehatan swalayan pertama di Jerman muncul padanya. Hari ini, dia multimiliarder. Mengapa dia senang menjadi sorotan? Pada tahun-tahun awal itu hanya tentang menempatkan nama Rossmann di luar sana. Jadi ketika saya diundang ke sebuah acara talk-show di acara regional kecil, saya suka pergi karena saya berpikir: "publisitas gratis untuk perusahaan saya". Tetapi dua atau tiga tahun yang lalu saya mulai memahami bahwa pria yang sedikit botak ini, yang giginya tidak sempurna, haus akan pengakuan bagi dirinya sendiri. Kala itu saya pikir saya memasuki pusat perhatian untuk mempromosikan perusahaan. Namun semua orang merasionalkan motif mereka, dan saya juga. Dalam empat puluh tahun, Rossmann menjadi rantai drugstore paling mencetak laba di Eropa, dengan toko-toko di enam negara. Pada 2018, total 56 ribu orang bekerja untuk rantai drugstore itu. Rossmann aktif di bidang bisnis lain juga, dan ia berspekulasi di pasar saham. Masukkan 10.000 lagi...Tepat. Batasnya 36,75 ... Oke, terima kasih. Sampai jumpa. Saya punya beberapa investasi ekuitas pribadi. Yang terbesar bernilai antara 80 dan 100 juta, tergantung pada harga bursa. Jadi itu angka yang adil. Dan kemudian ada juga andil saham... di berbagai industri. Untuk merinci itu semua akan membosankan. Anda masih masuk kategori UKM - Usaha Kecil Menengah. Mengapa menurut Anda begitu? Saya tidak begitu tahu. Dengan 55.000 karyawan, Anda bukan lagi perusahaan menengah. Anda berada di liga lain. Kami kembali ke Manager Magazine di Hamburg di mana foto-foto untuk edisi khusus sedang dipilih. Daftar orang-orang Jerman terkaya mencakup banyak pemilik perusahaan dari apa yang disebut bisnis kelas menengah. Ini adalah kekhasan Jerman. Dan itu bukan satu-satunya. Kisah sampul untuk topik saat ini adalah tahun lalu, terjadi hujan uang pada orang super kaya Jerman. Di atas sini kita memiliki Schaeffler, ibu pemimpin dan putranya: yang kini ia memiliki 80 persen, sang ibu hanya memegang 20. Schaeffler telah berada di puncak daftar kami selama bertahun-tahun. Saya harus memeriksa dengan tepat berapa nilainya, tapi sekitar 20 miliar, ditambah atau dikurangi sedikit. Di bawah sini kita memiliki Ibu Bagel-Trah, kepala klan Henkel. Ia orang nomor satu, dan sejauh ini satu-satunya wanita yang menjadi Ketua Dewan Pengawas pada satu dari 30 perusahaan DAX. Ekonomi Jerman masih sangat didominasi oleh pria. Yang menarik adalah jika Anda membandingkan daftar kami di sini, dengan daftar orang yang sangat kaya di Amerika Serikat, kami memiliki banyak uang lama; perusahaan lama yang telah berdiri selama beberapa dekade. Di Amerika Serikat, Anda memiliki semua pemuda - mulai dari Facebook, Google, Snapchat, dsb - yang telah menggelembung ke bagian atas daftar. Kami tidak memiliki hal semacam itu di sini. Dan dibandingkan dengan negara lain, Jerman sangat segan menunjukkan kekayaan mereka. Sangat sedikit orang Jerman yang berlayar dengan kapal seperti ini. Biasanya itu orang Amerika, Rusia, Cina, dan sebagainya. Di sini Anda tidak menunjukkan uang Anda. Anda mungkin punya berbagai rumah, villa, dan lainnya. Tapi paling mungkin ada Volkswagen yang diparkir di depan rumah. Tampaknya, rata-rata orang ultrakaya Jerman jarang mencolok. Tidak seperti di Amerika Serikat, atlet, aktor dan tokoh televisi, jarang masuk dalam daftar Jerman. Meskipun kami berusaha sepenuh hati dan tenaga memprediksi kekayaan ini, jumlahnya mungkin jauh lebih besar. Terutama jika kita berbicara tentang real estate perkotaan, di mana harga-harga telah meledak selama 10 tahun terakhir. Banyak orang menggandakan aset properti mereka. Jadi, jika Anda mulai dengan lima miliar di pasar itu, Anda mungkin memiliki 10 atau 15 miliar hari ini. Uang menghasilkan uang! Tapi sementara kekayaan orang Jerman kaya telah meledak sejak krisis keuangan - karena kenaikan nilai real estate, saham dan aset - mereka yang berpenghasilan rata-rata harus menelan kerugian. Bahkan lembaga ekonomi liberal pun prihatin tentang ketimpangan sosial di Jerman. Bagaimana orang super kaya melihat perbedaan ini? Judul berita di koran berbunyi: "Orang kaya semakin kaya dan semakin kaya." Yang adalah benar. Orang kaya semakin kaya. Itu adalah benar. Tapi itu juga salah. Ada 20 juta warga di Jerman, yang memiliki aset bernilai antara 100.000 hingga satu juta. Jadi jutaan orang menjadi semakin kaya. Sekarang, orang kaya semakin kaya bahkan lebih cepat, karena salah satu faktor mungkin mereka bisa mendedikasikan lebih banyak waktu untuk menambah kekayaan mereka. Sahra Wagenknecht dari partai Kiri mungkin akan berkata: "pisahkan kekayaan Anda!" Tapi respons saya adalah saya juga berbuat hal bagi dunia tempat saya tinggal, saya tidak hanya mengambil. Meskipun saya bukan seorang sosialis. Saya hanya dapat melakukan hal-hal karena saya memiliki 'hal-hal'. Michael Otto adalah salah satu orang kaya yang kekayaannya meningkat. Ia berhasil mengalihkan bisnis pesanan lewat paketnya menjadi perusahaan digital. Lebih dari dua puluh perusahaan sekarang milik grup Otto. Kami ingin tahu bagaimana Otto melihat debat tentang kaya dan miskin. Apakah yang kaya memahami kekhawatiran yang miskin? Saat orang-orang membahas orang-orang di atas yang tidak mengerti mereka yang di bawah, saya tidak akan mengatakan itu berlaku untuk saya. Karena saya tidak dilahirkan kaya. Saya datang ke Hamburg sebagai pengungsi dari Prusia Barat dan ayah saya harus memulai dari awal. Itu sebabnya saya benar-benar mengerti orang yang hidup dalam kemiskinan saat ini. Di sisi lain, saya juga mengatakan jika Jerman mendapatkan semakin banyak jutawan, artinya orang-orang dengan usaha kecil atau menengah - karena umumnya para jutawan itu membangun bisnis yang mereka jalankan sekarang - maka saya pikir itu hebat. Karena mereka adalah orang-orang yang menciptakan pekerjaan. Bagi saya, itu yang penting dalam perdebatan ini. Kita harus berfokus pada hal itu, dan bukan kaya versus miskin. Memastikan bahwa kekayaan keluarga kaya dapat meningkat meskipun tingkat bunga nol persen saat ini adalah misi perusahaan Christian von Bechtolsheim. ...Terima kasih atas undangannya. Untuk mengubah sedikit nuansa secara singkat... Setiap hari, ia dan karyawannya mengirimkan peluang investasi dari bank dan entitas lain yang diberikan secara eksklusif kepada kliennya yang kaya. Ini adalah cara untuk menyajikan penawaran kepada beberapa klien yang berharga dan Focam. Adilkah orang-orang kaya Jerman bisa menambah kekayaannya, sementara penduduk lainnya tertinggal di belakang? Saya pikir sulit untuk menerapkan istilah seperti kesamaan dan keadilan dalam hal distribusi kekayaan. Saya akan berkata, di sini di Jerman kami lebih baik daripada sebelumnya, dan orang-orang di sini hidup dalam keadaan di atas rata-rata. Bagaimana pun, kami harus memastikan kesenjangan antara kaya dan miskin tidak menjadi terlalu lebar, karena kami tidak ingin konflik sosial seperti di Amerika Serikat atau Amerika Latin terjadi di sini. Perusahaan von Bechtolsheim Focam, adalah apa yang disebut 'multi-family office'. Family office mengurus kebutuhan keluarga yang sangat kaya, mengelola dan meningkatkan aset mereka. Baru ada gunanya untuk menggunakan jasa family office jika kekayaan Anda berada di atas 30 juta Euro. Siapa yang mampu membayar hal seperti itu? Nah, family office seperti milik kami jelas tidak dapat membicarakan klien-kliennya. Kami memiliki keluarga-keluarga bisnis Jerman yang terkenal. Itu adalah profil khas klien kami. Seseorang yang adalah atau pernah menjadi pemilik perusahaan berpikir berbeda dengan seseorang yang menempuh hidupnya sebagai karyawan. Atau mereka adalah keluarga yang sudah lama memiliki uang, jadi kekayaan bisa dikatakan ada dalam gen mereka. Dan kemudian ada keluarga, yang baru saja memasuki kekayaan mereka - yang masih memompa dengan energi kewirausahaan. Mereka biasanya sangat berbeda dari ahli waris. Rainer Schaller tampaknya memiliki banyak energi wirausaha ini. Perusahaannya adalah McFit, rantai studio kebugaran terbesar di Eropa. Kantor pusatnya berada di pabrik roti tua di Berlin. Rainer Schaller memulai itu dari kecil. Tapi hari ini, kekayaannya bernilai 250 juta Euro. Saya lahir di dekat Bamberg dan besar di sebuah desa kecil. Biasanya di desa, Anda melakukan olahraga yang tersedia, dan ketika saya berusia sekitar 15, 16 tahun, panutan saya adalah Arnold Schwarzenegger dan Stallone. Itulah mengapa saya masuk ke dunia fitness. Schaller lulus sekolah menengah lalu menyelesaikan magang wiraniaga, dan menjadi manajer tiga supermarket. Kemudian dia memutuskan untuk memulai sesuatu yang baru. Ketika saya berusia 25 tahun saya memutuskan untuk beralih ke industri jasa kebugaran. Ide saya adalah membuka pusat kebugaran di mana semua orang bisa berlatih, terlepas dari penghasilannya. Itu ide awalnya. Dan saya punya tujuan besar. Saya ingin menjadi orang nomor satu di Eropa. Tapi hanya itu yang saya miliki, saya tidak memiliki biaya. Saat itu tahun 1997 ketika Schaller membuka studio kebugaran pertamanya di Würzburg dekat dengan desa asalnya. Ia memanfaatkan sejumlah metode yang tidak biasa. Würzburg adalah langkah besar bagi saya. Saya membuka studio kebugaran pertama saya di sana dengan slogan "Sekarang juga di Würzburg!" yang dilihat orang sebagai lelucon pemasaran. Karena konsumen datang kepada saya dan bertanya di mana lagi kami memiliki studio. Jadi saya memikirkannya dan membuat lelucon pemasaran berikutnya: "Segera juga di Erlangen." Itu berhasil dan juga menempatkan saya di jalur pergi dari Würzburg ke Erlangen. Dan kemudian itu berkembang dari sana. Sepuluh tahun kemudian Rainer Schaller mencapai tujuannya. Dia Nomor Satu di Eropa dan masih terus berekspansi. McFit sekarang memiliki 10 perusahaan jasa kebugaran serta agensi modelnya sendiri. Sementara itu Schaller bersiap-siap untuk membuka studio kebugaran di Amerika Serikat. Menjadi Nomor 1 jauh lebih mudah daripada tetap Nomor 1. Saya pikir jika Anda ingin sukses, Anda harus menjadi sedikit binatang alfa dalam diri Anda. Investor mungkin akan memilih merek dan berkata, “Oke, itu bisa berhasil. Saya suka,” meskipun mungkin dua langkah terlalu jauh bagi banyak orang. Tetapi jika seseorang tidak ingin terlibat dengan kami karena itu, apa masalahnya! Saya yakin, hal yang lebih mudah bagi Anda jika Anda telah berjuang sampai ke puncak dan meraih kesuksesan. Klien merasakannya, dan begitu juga mitra-mitra. Saya pikir itu situasi kami. Itulah sebabnya saya juga bisa membayangkan seseorang yang mewarisi sesuatu, atau mengambil alih - atau bahkan harus mengambil alih perusahaan pada generasi kedua atau ketiga - akan memiliki waktu yang jauh lebih sulit. Tetapi ada banyak ahli waris di Jerman. Kekayaan besar dan ribuan perusahaan telah diwariskan dari satu generasi ke generasi berikutnya. Ada ahli waris yang tidak mau, dan lainnya, yang tidak boleh mengambil alih bisnis orang tua mereka. Penerus memiliki makna sangat penting di antara mereka yang terkaya di Jerman. Michael Otto mewarisi perusahaan pengiriman barang lewat paket dari ayahnya dan secara sukses mengelolanya. Akan lebih sulit bagi anak-anak saya, karena kini Grup Otto memiliki 123 perusahaan di lebih dari 30 negara. Saya mengenal setiap perusahaan, apakah karena saya terlibat ketika itu didirikan atau karena saya memimpin negosiasi pengambilalihan. Tapi anak-anak saya belum mengenal banyak perusahaan. Anak-anak Otto memilih menentang penerusan secara langsung. Saya pikir penting untuk memberi anak-anak pilihan, tanpa menekan mereka, jadi Anda tidak memaksa mereka memasuki sebuah peran. Saya pikir kesalahan itu sering terjadi dan saya katakan itu buruk bagi anak-anak dan perusahaan. Jadi sekarang kita punya satu titik penjualan? Burgwedel? Apa kamu agak lelah? Ya. Sudah beberapa hari ini melelahkan. Dalam 10 menit saya akan fit lagi. Itu hanya 'bum, bum, bum'. Dirk Rossmann juga menghabiskan waktu lama mempertimbangkan siapa akan menjadi penggantinya. Itu bukan secara tepat bagian dari kisaran drugstore. Trennya sudah berlalu. Itu besar pada tahun 2016, sudah menurun tahun 2017, dan itu mengalami stagnasi pada 2018. Christian von Bechtolsheim telah melihat banyak perselisihan warisan dalam keluarga kaya. Adalah tugasnya untuk melestarikan aset keluarga dan melindunginya dari segala macam bahaya. Mempertahankan kekayaan keluarga selama beberapa generasi adalah sangat sulit, karena ia terancam terbagi-bagi melalui warisan, dari perselisihan kekayaan, dari pengambilalihan, dari perang, atau hanya dari kebodohan. Kebanyakan keluarga akan mengalami satu atau semua ini terjadi pada mereka. Hanya segelintir keluarga yang berhasil bertahan kurang lebih selama berabad-abad, tapi mereka yang berada di atas telah berganti berkali-kali. Von Bechtolsheim berbicara dari pengalaman pribadi. Sejarah keluarganya sendiri sudah ada sejak 900 tahun yang lalu. Saya punya nama sangat panjang, setidaknya pada akte kelahiran saya. Baron von Bechtolsheim adalah penerus tidak langsung dari Fugger, keluarga terkaya dalam sejarah Jerman. Kapan ‘kekayaan’ dimulai menurut Anda? Hampir bangkrut... Tahun-tahun dibanjiri uang... dan tahun-tahun ketika banyak yang hilang. Dia membawa kami ke pondok berburu keluarga Bechtolsheim di Thüringen. Dikira pondok itu akan hilang selamanya selama pembagian Jerman. Rumah itu dibangun pada tahun 1892 sebagai pondok berburu untuk paman buyut saya. Ini sudah milik keluarga sejak itu, kecuali untuk interval singkat. Itu diambil alih pada tahun 1952, dan kemudian dikembalikan lagi pada 1992, dan sejak itu, saya pemiliknya. Pondok berburu keluarga selamat dari perampasan dan sosialisme tanpa banyak kerusakan. Itu yang menembak kakek buyut saya dan kemudian anjing saya mengunyah telinganya. Jadi dia tidak lagi memiliki kecantikan sebelumnya, tetapi itu terlalu berharga untuk tidak disimpan. Mereka dikendalikan oleh aset mereka sendiri. Menurut pendapat saya, itu tidak masuk akal mengingat kondisi yang saat ini kami miliki di Jerman. Kondisi di Jerman saat ini lebih menguntungkan daripada sebelumnya bagi orang kaya. Mereka membayar pajak jauh lebih sedikit daripada yang mereka lakukan 25 tahun yang lalu. Hanya sebagian kecil yang masih merasa terdorong untuk beremigrasi ke luar negeri. Pasal 14 Konstitusi Jerman berbunyi: “Properti mensyaratkan kewajiban. Penggunaannya juga akan bermanfaat bagi publik.” Apakah orang kaya di Jerman memenuhi tanggung jawab ini? Saya pikir penting jika Anda berhasil, jika Anda cukup beruntung telah mencapai tingkat kemakmuran dan kekayaan tertentu, untuk memberikan kembali sesuatu kepada masyarakat. Bagi saya, itu sudah pasti. Michael Otto adalah salah satu donor terbesar Jerman. Uangnya membantu mendanai sektor lingkungan, budaya, dan sosial. Seperti kebanyakan orang kaya, ia lebih suka memutuskan sendiri untuk apa ia membelanjakan uangnya, daripada menyerahkannya pada negara. Otto seperti orang-orang kaya Jerman lainnya menyumbang jutaan untuk pembangunan Elbphilharmonie di Hamburg. Di Jerman, orang kaya suka menyumbang, dan ini membuat kontribusi penting bagi masyarakat dan kehidupan publik. Tapi secara umum, mereka menentang usul mendistribusikan kembali kekayaan lewat pajak lebih tinggi Jika bisnis akan memenuhi semua tuntutan dari katakanlah Ibu Wagenknecht dari Partai Kiri, maka jutaan orang akan bahagia dan segalanya akan baik untuk sementara. Karena jutaan orang akan memiliki lebih banyak uang. Tapi pembagian kembali kekayaan yang sejati tak pernah membawa keadilan sosial lebih besar dalam jangka panjang, tidak dalam sistem politik mana pun yang telah mencobanya. Itu menyebabkan pemiskinan negara-negara ini. Christian von Bechtolsheim melihat pajak lebih tinggi bagi orang kaya juga berbahaya. Saya tidak berpikir banyak tentang apa yang disebut "pajak orang kaya" karena dua alasan: Pertama, terminologi sendiri yang memberi stigma, dan kami di Jerman harus menghindari itu. Dan kedua, "pajak kaya" tidak akan ada gunanya. Sebaliknya: itu akan memotong tulang punggung ekonomi Jerman, karena orang kaya khas Jerman adalah pemilik bisnis menengah. Mereka menjadi tulang punggung ekonomi Jerman dan jika kami ingin menghancurkannya, kami tak bisa salahkan siapa pun selain kami sendiri. Edisi khusus siap. Hal-hal pada dasarnya tetap sama. Orang yang kaya punya beberapa miliar lebih banyak. Orang terkaya yang mencakup 1 persen penduduk Jerman sekarang memiliki kekayaan pribadi senilai 25 persen dari aset negara, sementara 25 persen orang dewasa tidak memiliki kekayaan, atau dililit utang. Untuk laporan ini kami tidak pernah mendapat akses nyata ke dunia orang Jerman terkaya. Mereka lebih suka terbang diam-diam di bawah radar, jauh dari mata publik, hingga acara penghargaan berikutnya.

All right, so by now you've gotten a taste for the rental income game, right? Naturally, you start to think, how do I get more of this? And the answer is becoming a BRR investor at level four. And trust us, this strategy really does make the money printer go. So here's how it works. All right. So, imagine you're driving around in a great neighborhood and you spot a house that's worn down and outdated. Most people would pass without a second glance, but you see potential. So, you buy that house for well below market value. You spend a few months upgrading it. Nothing too fancy, but enough to turn it into a place that people want to live in. And just like before, you rent it out. But this time, something's different. Now that the property looks better and brings in more income, it's actually worth a lot more than what you bought it for. And here's the best part. You can actually go back to the bank now, refinance the property as its new value, and come out with most or even all of the cash you originally put into it. You still own the property, so it's adding to your net worth. It's still generating income for you with rent, but now you've got your money back, and you can do it all over again. This is the BRRR strategy. Buy, renovate, rent, refinance, repeat. It's one of the fastest ways to build a real estate portfolio because instead of waiting and saving, you're using the value you just created to fund your next move. At this level, you're not just making money. You've unlocked a system that literally creates wealth. But at some point, you start to notice something, right? Your residential properties are fine. They're steady, but you're getting tired of dealing with late night plumbing issues, missed rent, and annoying tenants. Then you meet somebody who's in commercial real estate. They don't lease to people, they lease to businesses. These kinds of investors are commercial specialists. That is at level five. When you're dealing in commercial real estate, your tenants aren't calling about clogged drains. They're signing 5-year contracts with automatic rent increases. In some cases, the tenant, not the landlord, pays for property taxes, insurance, and maintenance. That means higher margins and more predictable income for the owner. So, you take the leap. You buy a small office building, a warehouse, and a retail strip with three units. The leases are longer, the income is higher, and the tenants, well, they treat the space like it's their own because it's their business. That's the beauty of commercial real estate. You're not just collecting rent. You're locking in revenue for years at a time with fewer management headaches. That is long-term thinking. Okay, so there's definitely more to real estate than just homes and apartments, right? The deeper you go, the more it becomes clear. Most people are only scratching the surface. At level six, you start exploring those opportunities and become a specialty investor. So, at this stage, smart investors start looking into places that others ignore. mobile home parks, farmland, RV parks, self storage, billboards, even laundromats. These properties aren't glamorous, no, but they're simple, predictable, and often wildly profitable. Take mobile home parks for example. Residents pay rent to park there, and because they own their homes, they tend to stay for years. In many of these deals, investors don't own the homes, they just own the land underneath. So simply put, expenses are low and cash flow is strong. Self storage is another big one. There's no tenants living on site. The upkeep is minimal and there's no toilets, so it's one of the easiest real estate assets to automate. In fact, the self-s storage industry has delivered an average annual return of over 16% over the last 25 years. And then there's farmland. Quiet, stable, and surprisingly consistent. Everybody needs food, and there's more people every day. So, the land produces income and it appreciates over time. Just look at Bill Gates. There's probably a reason that he's the biggest owner of farmland in the US. This level is all about realizing that wealth doesn't always look like a luxury high-rise. Sometimes it looks like a cornfield, a gravel lot, or 40 rows of storage lockers. But look, okay, here's the thing. You can only buy so many properties on your own. Eventually, you're going to hit a wall. Not because you're out of ideas, but because you're out of cash and you can't even borrow anymore. But don't worry. If you decide to go into level seven, real estate mogul, this is exactly where things get interesting. So, instead of waiting years to save up, you can raise money from other people to buy properties. They find a great deal, usually something big like an apartment complex or even entire malls, and then bring in investors to help fund the purchase. You handle the work. You find the deal, negotiate the price, set up the legal structure, and manage the property. The investors just write the checks and collect returns. It's like a 1 + 1 equals 11 kind of situation. You build equity and earn fees without putting in all of the money yourself. And your investors get access to deals they couldn't do on their own without having to deal with tenants or toilets. So, at the end of the day, you could go it alone and keep it simple, or you could turn real estate into a team sport and win the big game. And as the old saying goes, alone we can do so little. Together, we can do so much. In your own wealth creation journey, whether it's through real estate or something else, you have to apply the same kinds of mindset. This is why we created the Alux app to give our community the chance to learn directly from the world's foremost experts on wealth creation. It's already helped hundreds of thousands of people become more successful, and we're confident it can change your life, too. So, download the app in your app store, scan the QR code on screen, and you'll get 25% off your yearly plan. So, yeah, check it out, my friend. You won't regret it, and I'll be right there with you on the inside. Let's move on, shall we? So, now that you've built the kind of real estate empire that would make the Monopoly man jealous, at a certain point, you have to realize you spend years looking for the perfect property to buy and you never find it. Right? At level eight, that's not a problem because you become a real estate developer. If your perfect property doesn't exist yet, well, you can always just build it yourself. Now, obviously, it's much easier to buy a home than it is to build one. It takes permits and planning. contractor's capital lots of time. But just imagine how tough it is to build a stadium or a mall. If you do it right, though, it could be the kind of move that sets you and your grandkids and your grandkids grandkids up for life. Now, it usually starts with a piece of dirt in a location that's about to grow. Maybe a new highway is about to be built or a big company announces they're opening a facility nearby. The land might look empty right now, but as a developer, you see what it could be. With the right timing and execution, a wellplanned project can 10x your original investment once it's finished. This is how some of the biggest fortunes in real estate are made. Not by buying what's already profitable, but by turning raw land into something the world is hungry for. All right, so if you wanted to reach the top of the real estate game, you pretty much made it by this point. But there is one more strategy you could implement, and it's a little bit crazy, like a maniacal evil genius. But it's currently making ultra-wealthy people crazy rich and crazy powerful. At level 9, you become too big to fail. You see, at the top, the real estate food chain are institutional investors. Take Black Rockck for example, a massive fund backed by literally tens of trillions of dollars in capital. These guys aren't looking for a home to raise the kids in. They're buying thousands of homes in bulk. Sometimes scooping up entire neighborhoods before they ever hit the public market. This is part of the reason why buying a home feels impossible these days. To the average person, it looks like a housing shortage. To Black Rockck and to others like them, it's a strategy. Now, you would think these funds would be really careful about what they buy because if the real estate market crashes, well, they are the market. But here's the thing, okay? These funds are not afraid to make mistakes, to buy aggressively, and to get in debt. That's because they know that if and when the crash comes, the government is 100% going to bail them out. Otherwise, the ripple effect could destabilize everything from local housing to the entire financial system. So, simply put, there's effectively no downside for them. They can just get insanely rich without any consequences. At this level, real estate is not about cash flow anymore. It is about power. We're going to wrap this one up here today. Hopefully, you got a good grasp of how people are getting rich with real estate and how you can go from zero to crazy evil genius rich as well. If you enjoyed this video, subscribe and hit that bell icon below so you don't miss any future ones. We'll be making some more deep dives like this about other asset classes soon. So, with that said, we'll see you back here next time. Until then, take care, my friend. [Music]

- Why Marriott, Hilton and Hyatt Don’t Actually Own Most of Their Hotels  | WSJ The Economics Of: Kind: captions Language: en - [Narrator] There's something going on in the hotel industry. Just look at this one block in Chicago. These might seem like three different hotels, but they're actually all Marriott. Over the past 20 years, Marriott has more than tripled in size. And we can trace this boom back to a decision Marriott made decades ago, to move away from the real estate business and instead, commodify its name. - Well, of course, as you might imagine, we think we're the best. We are the largest, 8,700 hotels in 139 countries and territories, really showing expertise around the world for what we think hospitality means. - [Narrator] And you can see the same happening for competitors like Hilton, and now Hyatt, because they're also following a similar playbook. - It's commonplace to actually invest behind real estate in order to build a brand, and then over time, sell down real estate. We wanted to do it in a very deliberate way. - [Narrator] With this business model, the brands don't have to run most of the hotels that fly their flags, nor do they have to pay for them. In most cases, that responsibility falls on people like this guy. - So independent owner-operators like us generally own the hotels and employ the team members. Most people think that they are Marriott employees. They're generally MCR employees. - [Narrator] So why did this shift happen, and how does it affect the customers? This is the economics of hotel chains. The business today looks like this. These are the names you're familiar with, Marriott, Hilton, Hyatt. And these are the players who actually front the money to buy the real estate, hire the employees, and run the day-to-day operations. Owners pay to use these big hotel names, referred to as flags in the industry, and it's a departure from the tried and true strategy of decades before. - [Tyler] If you go back to the 50s, everything was owned, operated, and flagged by the same person. - [Narrator] MCR is the third largest hotel owner-operator behind these two real estate investment trusts. - We get all the revenue, and then we get whatever profits are left over. And we pay Marriott or Hilton a franchise fee to be part of their system. - [Narrator] That means hotel brands mainly have money to gain with every new hotel. - Because they were not on the hook for the asset cost, they were able to scale up their brands much faster. - [Narrator] Moving to franchising removed a lot of the financial risks associated with real estate. - One of the things we realized as those hotels got more and more popular is that by building them all on balance sheet and with dealing with economic cycles, we were constrained. Our growth was constrained. - [Narrator] Now, Marriott and Hilton each own less than 1% of their properties, while Hyatt owns about 2%. But in shifting to this strategy, major hotel brands now have to focus on a new customer. - The owner is the one that puts the brand onto their hotel, and that's what drives franchise fees to the brands. - [Narrator] Those fees can amount to anywhere from 5 to 15% of what the property brings in. So the brands have to prove that having the name is worth it. Hotel brands say. - It more than pays for itself. - [Narrator] Because in addition to data on how lobby should look and what amenities to have, they also provide data to help the owner get the most money for each room each night. - At the end of the day, you are saying, "What will the market bear?" When there's a Taylor Swift concert coming into a market, we see the rates literally out to 40 miles away go crazy. - [Narrator] In dynamic cities, the price of a room could change multiple times in one day. - We get questions all the time and they say, "I was gonna book for $199, and now the rate just went up to $249." You know, then you should have booked at the 199. The prices are fleeting. - Every one of our full-service hotels has a revenue manager, and they take account of what's going on in that particular marketplace. - [Narrator] And these systems also calculate rates that optimize for the hotel's bottom line. - [Tyler] We don't wanna sell out our hotels just on a Tuesday because then that leaves us exposed on Wednesday. So we may sell you that room at an extraordinarily high price because what we really want is for you to book Monday to Wednesday or Tuesday to Thursday. - [Narrator] This helps make up for less profitable days. - We're giving the rooms away on Sundays. It is the worst night of the week because business travel does not really occur on Sundays and neither does leisure travel. - [Narrator] Flying a flag can also help owners target wider pools of hotel customers, with better fees for booking platforms like Expedia and Booking.com. - So if you buy a hotel room via Expedia for a Marriott hotel, we pay a lower Expedia fee. We, the owner. - [Narrator] But analysts say an even bigger draw, "Our hotel loyalty programs." Marriott and Hilton each have over 180 million members. Hyatt has more than 40 million. And thanks to points, guests are incentivized to shop within these programs. - The points have essentially become currency. - [Narrator] Which can help increase the customer base for flagged hotels in smaller markets. - If you're in a secondary or tertiary market, you generally fly a flag because that brings people to your building 'cause they want the points. The idea is you earn the points in Corpus Christi or in Yuma, Arizona, and then you redeem them in Miami and New York. - [Narrator] Today, two-thirds of all hotels in the US are branded, but the franchise model isn't always beneficial. For owners in high-demand markets, like those with more leisure travel, independence might be a better option. - The highest performing hotels in Manhattan are independent hotels. You have a greater elasticity of demand. So usually, the highest performing hotels are smaller. - [Narrator] But brands do operate some properties mainly in this tier, whereas franchising mostly happens in these. - Those operations have dramatically fewer touch points and offerings by virtue of the fact that they're designed for a person who wants a great room to stay in, a quick option for breakfast, but they don't need other things. They don't need a ballroom. They don't need a specialty restaurant. They don't need a spa. - [Narrator] In the luxury tier, those amenities are expected, creating more complex logistics. That's why most luxury hotels are still operated by the hotel brand itself. - With respect to most other full service and luxury properties and resorts, we would prefer to manage because we wanna control all aspects of the delivery of the guest experience. - Specifically at the luxury end, we do overwhelmingly manage those hotels, and we wouldn't, for example, be talking about franchising an EDITION Hotel. What having this range of brands gives us the ability to appeal to our customer for every experience and location that they want, and that is really important for Bonvoy. - [Narrator] In some rare cases, hotel brands will still strategically purchase a property. Marriott bought this W Hotel in New York to use it as an incubator for new ideas and designs, and Hyatt purchased Hotel Irvine. - We bought the hotel. We completely renovated it. We will eventually sell that hotel. - [Narrator] But by and large, more hotels are outsourcing the owning and operating increasing the volume of branded properties offered to consumers today. - The customer now has more choices. They have more places to both earn and redeem. But what you have seen in the past 25 years is a consolidation. There are really three or four big hotel flag families now. - [Narrator] And looking at the properties in construction, the number of branded hotels is only set to increase.

- The Difference Between Wealth Management and Asset Management: Kind: captions Language: en OK so now you've been at JP Morgan for about 25 years. Yes. So and now you run one of the most important parts of JP Morgan which as I say is the asset and wealth management business for people that aren't that familiar with wealth management. What actually is wealth management and how is that different than asset management. Great question. The two are often used interchangeably. But but but there they have distinctions. Asset management business is where we manage money on behalf of individuals institutions sovereign wealth funds pension funds. We manage them in mutual funds. We manage them an ETF. We manage them in single stock single bonds hedge funds private equity and the like. And that is the heart of the fiduciary business that we run here at JP Morgan. Wealth management is that plus understanding someone's entire balance sheet. So for the individuals where we manage money we also help them with their mortgage. We help them with a loan that they might need. We help them with their basic credit card. And so wealth management is trying to help someone with their entire life both their assets and their liabilities their planning their gifting the legacy that they want to leave for their families. The 529 plans they need to prepare to get their kids to go through college. And it's a great it's a great insight into people's you know entire journey. Now many organizations like J.P. Morgan to have wealth management businesses some are bigger than some are smaller. But basically you're managing money for and doing other things for wealthy people more or less. Is that fairly right for wealthy people. Although you know many of the successful wealth management firms today have figured out how to take all of those great learnings for what they do with very wealthy people and also package them for people who are have their first paycheck. And they want to be able to save a little bit of money or want to have access to things that maybe they wouldn't normally have. And so we've been able to take things like what we do for a super wealthy family package it into a bite size where you walk into a chase branch and you're able to get some of that some of the same advice. And so it's it's I think it's opening up the world to be able to help people. And you know the most important thing is to be able to save early. And if someone can be there to help you through that you know that's that's one of the most important things. If you look at an average investment in the world if you just look over the past 20 years take a balanced portfolio. It's about six point four percent average annual return for people that generally manage money. The problem is most individuals actual return is less than 3 percent. So it's less than half of that. Why. Because they make emotional decisions when markets are one way or another and they get caught up in the hype of things. And so it's super important to have that advice as early on as we can give it. And I think you know that that's the rewarding part about about this business is being able to try to help people through all of those different journeys that they have.

- Investing In Wine: A True Alternative Investment with Anthony Zhang of Vinovest: Kind: captions Language: en Our mission at Resilient Advisor is to have a significant impact on the retirement crisis by educating and empowering financial advisors to better serve their clients. We bring you industry thought leaders and experts to help you make a difference in the lives of your clients. Thanks for tuning in to this episode of The Resilient Advisor. My name is Jay Coulter. If I told you that there was an investment that had returned a thousand percent over the S&P 500 for the past 20 years while having about a third of the volatility, I'm sure that would catch your attention. You would probably also think that it was some arcane cryptocurrency or gold or silver, but it's not. Today I'm speaking with Anthony Zhang, the CEO and co-founder of a company called Vinovest. They are a fintech platform that enables consumers to invest in wine. Anthony, thanks for coming on to tell your story. Jay, thanks so much for having me on board. I'm excited to chat. Well, this is unique. Like I told you in our pre-call, normally if I'm interviewing a large-cap growth manager, I I'm I'm a pretty good cadence on how to execute that type of interview. So, let's start with the investment case. What do you think the investment case for wine is? Absolutely. So, I think with wine, there's a few key aspects, right? Um A, it's an alternative asset that has low correlation with the market. B, it also has low volatility. So, you're getting these really strong returns on their own, but they're also really strong risk-adjusted returns. And I think number three is that it is a part of I think a glo- a growing trend of collectibles becoming a bigger and bigger part of mainstream investing. So, we've got this other tailwind that I believe will start to push wine prices up higher and higher into the future. Uh you're seeing a lot of emerging countries that have a large uh wealthy millennial populations, whether it be uh China or India or um a lot of other cities in um in Asia where people are starting to want to consume these luxury goods. And especially when we're talking about investment-quality wines, these wineries, they can't make any more grape juice. They're acquiring the same plot of land for decades, and because of also climate change, it's getting hotter and hotter, the yields are getting lower and lower each year, and they're producing wine at lower quantities when there's greater global demand for those wines. Okay. So, to be clear, they're making less wine today than they did a decade ago? Yeah, because their harvests are getting hotter and hotter, yields are getting lower, and unfortunately, they don't want to be able to um you know, farm other places that may be unsuitable for wine because that'll decrease the overall quality. Okay. So, when I think of wine, uh I'm not a wine connoisseur, but I think of the $20 bottle of wine I get at the grocery store. I have a feeling that's not what you're talking about. Could you kind of help viewers and listeners conceptualize the type of wine you're talking about? Yeah, so not quite grocery store wine. The wine that we're talking about usually is um on allocation or on a mailing list. Uh wine that typically is pretty hard to get your hands on because it either A is uh so rare or B is so expensive. So, usually the wines that we're talking about that are investment-quality, anywhere between 200 bucks a bottle and can get up to, you know, fetching hundreds of thousands dollars a bottle. Uh but you know, because there is a big range, it also gives us flexibility when we're working with different clients, designing their portfolios for different needs, different timelines, as well as different price points. Um but I think to unite the definition of investment-grade wine, there's a few key criteria. And I think number one is going to be the scarcity aspect. Um they're not mass-produced. They are um very much so uh place-driven. Uh they're have strong brand equity as well so that you can recognize uh this wine over others. And then number three is going to be that they're ageable. So, these are wines that are not meant to really be consumed on day one. Uh although you can, they'll still be delicious. But I think the important part of it is that uh wine ages, right? A 10-year-old bottle of wine tastes different than a 30-year-old bottle of wine. And wine collectors and enthusiasts and uh fine-dining establishments want to be able to allow consumers to experience that rare 30, 40, 50-year bottle, which is why they become much more sought after um when they're older. So, what are your thoughts around wine as an inflation hedge given the environment that we're in and will be in for a while? I think that's why a lot of advisors are coming to us because um there aren't too many other places where you can find inflation hedges. And especially when you're looking at the historical performance of this asset class, uh harkening back to say '08, '09, or even the dot-com crash, when we were looking at huge drawdowns in global equities, you know, 40, 50% during those two time periods, wine was only down around 10%. So, it's proved through two cycles that it has been a pretty strong hedge uh against recessions and also against It's really hard to do a dividend discount model on a bottle of wine. It's going to be really hard to put a framework around it for a financial advisor to come up with a price that matters. And I can already see the cynical advisor saying, "Well, this is a greater fool investment." Like you buy it today and hope that somebody else buys it later. I know that you have much more meat behind that in terms of how you value it today and how that appreciation would go. But for the cynical advisor listening, what do you say to them as it relates to how to price it today and how they could expect to generate returns on that investment? Yeah, I think for us, we're looking at the sales, right? The consumption of that bottle of wine. And thankfully, there is a robust secondary market, especially for these investment-quality wines, right? Um you're not going to see much retail on a uh you know, your bottle of two-buck chuck, but we're talking about a thousand-dollar bottle of Chateau Lafite. Um not only do you have strong secondary demand at all times, but you also have historicals to be able to look back on, right? Uh this wine has been producing for almost 150 years now, and you can see what an average year five Lafite looks like or a year 10 Lafite looks like or even a year 20. So, um you can really model that all out and be able to look ahead with pretty high confidence on what that future price may look like and be able to price it accordingly, get accurate mark-to-market on a weekly or monthly basis, and be able to confidently talk to your clients about what their returns are looking like. All right. And is your offering through financial advisors and direct to consumers? It's both. So, we have a both retail and advisor-facing platform. All right. So, before we get into the advisor-facing platform, let's go through a little role play. All right. So, I've have a $5 million portfolio. I manage my money myself, and I I like wine, let's say. And so, understand that there's a fine wine component to it, but you know, maybe I buy a $100 bottle of wine on special occasions. And I think, "Okay, I want to allocate ex- a a certain percentage of my portfolio to wine." Mhm. What's the process? What are the risks? And what's the experience? So, the process I think starts with knowing what you want to get out of this investment. Is this a five-year hold for you, a 10-year hold, 20-year hold? And then looking at what else you have in your portfolio, I think you want to determine how much risk you want to put on into this investment. Um whether it be from an allocation standpoint or from an asset level standpoint because just like stocks, there are your equivalent of, you know, blue-chips, stable, large-cap wines. Uh these will be wineries coming from usually areas that have been producing wine for a long time. They've got a lot of historical data, and the outlook for that region is very stable. On the other hand, if you want to go a little bit more speculative or um you know, have a little bit of a higher appetite for risk, there are your emerging regions or your wineries that have just gone over new ownership and are looking to really aggressively change their investment outlook. So, those wines can be part of a a diversified wine portfolio that can give you some risk as well as your sort of blue-chip stability for the asset class. Um so, that's really the process that we want to take the individual investor or the RA through to determine what's that best portfolio construction. Um because just like bonds, there are wines that can hit their uh their peak maturity periods in, say, five years versus 10 years versus 10 and 20 years. So, we've got that flexibility there. And then when we're looking at the risks of the asset class, number one is going to be storage because wine is um you know, it's a real living thing that can get damaged, right? If you are in a too hot of a condition, too cold of a condition, too much or too little humidity, or even something like uh exposing it to direct sunlight, all that can damage the wine and can render that investment to be close to zero. So, proper storage and insurance on that storage is going to be really crucial. Number two is going to be the provenance. So, where did the wine come from? A wine that we source from your basement is going to have a different retail value than a wine that's sourced direct from the winery sellers. So, at Vinovest we really care about A, where is this wine coming from? And B, how was it stored before we acquired it? And we want to make sure that both of those are in excellent condition to be able to ensure for the maximum resale value of that particular wine. - How The Ultra Rich Protect Their Privacy | Hidden Life of Billionaires | The Dark Luxury: Kind: captions Language: en [music] Most people believe that becoming [music] rich means becoming visible. Big houses, public attention, recognition everywhere you go. But what if the opposite is true? >> [music] >> What if the moment people become truly wealthy, they start trying to disappear? Because attention [music] doesn't just bring admiration, it brings risk. Strangers learn where you live. People track [music] where your children study. Business rivals watch every move. And suddenly, privacy becomes more valuable than luxury [music] itself. The world sees billionaires on magazine covers and social media. But the truth is, [music] the wealthiest families on Earth are often the ones you've never heard of. No interviews, no public exposure, no online presence, just quiet power [music] hidden behind carefully protected lives. In this film, we step inside the systems the ultra [music] rich use to protect their privacy, their families, and their freedom. Because at the highest levels of wealth, the [music] greatest luxury is not being known at all. And once you see how far they go to stay invisible, you'll never look at wealth the same way again. [music] The moment wealth becomes visible, attention follows. And attention at extreme levels of success is rarely harmless. For most people, recognition feels flattering. [music] Success is meant to be celebrated. Achievements are shared. Visibility feels rewarding. But for the ultra rich, visibility changes meaning. [music] It becomes exposure. The more successful someone becomes, the more people begin watching. Strangers grow curious. Media [music] searches for stories. Online audiences track movements. And not all attention comes [music] with good intentions. Wealth attracts admiration. But it also attracts [music] opportunists, scammers, extortion attempts, and individuals looking for access or advantage. Suddenly, personal information becomes [music] valuable. where you live, where your children study, where your [music] family vacations, even daily routines become data others try to exploit. Business competitors observe movements. Negotiations become harder when your lifestyle reveals success. Public visibility weakens [music] strategic privacy. This is the moment wealthy individuals begin to understand that exposure [music] carries consequences. What once felt like success starts feeling like vulnerability. Many [music] billionaires describe a turning point. A moment when public attention becomes overwhelming. [music] Random requests increase. Unwanted contact grows. Security concerns rise. Freedom quietly begins to shrink. Simple activities [music] become complicated. Dinner reservations attract attention. Travel requires planning. Even casual appearances invite cameras. And gradually priorities change. The goal is no longer recognition. The goal becomes [music] control. Control over personal space, control over family security, control over information. Because wealth offers many freedoms, [music] but public exposure can take them away. And this is why at extreme levels of success, the [music] smartest move is often not expansion, but disappearance. Because when wealth becomes visible, privacy becomes priceless. For [music] most people, a dream home is meant to be seen. Large entrances, open views, impressive architecture. A statement of success visible to everyone passing by. But for the ultra rich, home design follows a very different rule. The more valuable the life inside, the less attention the outside attracts. Many billionaire residences are intentionally hidden. Long private roads replace visible driveways. [music] Dense trees block outside views. Gates sit far from the main residence, [music] making it impossible to see the home from public roads. From the street, some properties appear almost ordinary. [music] No dramatic displays, no visible luxury, just privacy. Because security begins with invisibility. Inside, homes are designed in layers. Outer [music] perimeters prevent easy access. Security teams monitor entry points quietly. Technology tracks movement [music] long before visitors reach the door. Safe rooms, private exits, and protected areas are common, though rarely discussed publicly. Even property ownership is often concealed. Homes are purchased through companies [music] or trusts, making it difficult to trace the real owner. Location also matters. Ultra-wealthy [music] families choose neighborhoods where privacy is respected and protected. Communities with limited public access, private security, and neighbors who value discretion. [music] Daily life happens away from public attention. Children play without cameras. Family routines stay predictable and protected. Interestingly, many newly wealthy individuals initially choose visible mansions in famous locations. But over time, exposure becomes exhausting. Homes move farther away from attention. Design becomes quieter. Security becomes stronger because eventually the meaning of luxury changes. It is no longer about showing success. [music] It becomes about protecting normal life. And among the ultra rich, the most valuable homes are not the [music] ones everyone admires, but the ones nobody knows exist. For most people, travel is about visibility. Airport selfies, social media updates, crowded terminals, and excitement shared in real [music] time. But for the ultra rich, travel follows a completely different logic. Movement becomes one of the most vulnerable moments in daily life. Schedules [music] become predictable. Locations become traceable. And once patterns are visible, privacy disappears. Commercial airports expose [music] routines. Departure boards reveal destinations. Security lines slow movement. Crowds increase unpredictability. For individuals whose presence [music] attracts attention, these environments become risks rather than conveniences. This is why many ultra-wealthy individuals travel differently. Private aviation allows [music] schedules to remain flexible. Flights leave from private terminals away from public crowds. Passenger lists remain confidential. Arrivals and departures happen quietly, often unnoticed. Cars meet aircraft directly on private [music] runways, eliminating public exposure entirely. Even destinations [music] are chosen carefully. Hotels known for discretion are preferred over [music] famous locations. Private villas replace crowded resorts. Staff members are selected for confidentiality. Travel details are often shared only hours [music] before departure, sometimes only with essential personnel. Routes change regularly to avoid predictable [music] patterns. Social media silence becomes intentional. Photos are posted days [music] or weeks later, preventing realtime location tracking. Because for the ultra rich, the goal is not to show where they are, but to control who knows. Interestingly, many newly wealthy individuals [music] initially enjoy public travel experiences, luxury lounges, social posts, visible movement. But [music] over time, attention becomes exhausting. Freedom starts to feel restricted, and eventually convenience gives [music] way to privacy. Because true luxury travel is not about flying first class. It is about moving through the world without anyone knowing you were there at all. In today's world, privacy is no longer lost only [music] in public spaces. It disappears online first. For most people, sharing life digitally feels normal. Photos, locations, [music] daily routines, personal opinions, all posted without hesitation. [music] But for the ultra rich, the internet is one of the greatest threats to privacy. Every post reveals patterns. [music] Every tagged location exposes routines. Every shared [music] image reveals details strangers can analyze. A background view shows where someone lives. [music] A restaurant post reveals favorite places. A vacation photo announces [music] absence from home. And once information appears online, it becomes impossible to fully erase because data never truly disappears. This is why many billionaire families avoid social media entirely. Children are often raised without public online profiles. [music] Personal accounts remain private or non-existent. Professional teams monitor [music] digital presence constantly, removing leaked information, tracking mentions, and managing reputational risks before they spread. Even employees and household staff follow strict confidentiality rules. Photos inside homes are forbidden. [music] Locations are never shared publicly. Cyber security becomes part of daily protection. Encrypted communication, secure [music] networks, and identity protection systems guard personal information because digital exposure creates real world vulnerability. Interestingly, [music] newly wealthy individuals often enjoy online visibility. Success feels [music] worth sharing. But over time, exposure attracts unwanted attention. Scammers, [music] impersonators, and data breaches become common threats. And slowly, online presence begins [music] to shrink. Profiles go private. Posts become rare. Public visibility fades. Because eventually, the ultra rich understand that the most powerful online presence is sometimes no presence at all. In a world obsessed with being seen, true wealth learns the value of staying invisible. At ordinary levels of success, security feels optional. A home alarm, a secure car, occasional precautions. [music] But at extreme levels of wealth, protection becomes part of daily life. Because money does not only attract admiration. It attracts threats. Kidnapping attempts, stalking incidents, extortion schemes, and targeted crimes are realities the public rarely sees. And while these risks remain invisible to most people, the ultra rich [music] prepare for them constantly. Security teams become part of everyday routines. Not always visible, but always present. [music] Drivers trained in defensive movement. Close protection officers monitoring surroundings quietly. Advanced teams securing [music] locations before arrival. Even daily routes change regularly. Children travel with trained staff. [music] Schools coordinate security procedures discreetly. Family movements stay confidential. [music] Public appearances are carefully managed. Restaurants, events, and hotels receive prior security checks. Entry and exit routes are planned before arrival. Crowds are avoided when possible. Homes operate with layered protection systems. Cameras monitor [music] surroundings continuously. Access points remain controlled. Emergency response plans stay ready at all times. [music] Interestingly, many wealthy individuals initially resist this lifestyle. Constant protection can [music] feel restrictive. Freedom appears reduced, but after experiencing unwanted attention or threats, perspectives [music] change quickly. Safety becomes more important than convenience. And over time, protection becomes routine rather than intrusion. [music] Because at extreme wealth, risk management becomes responsibility. Not only for personal safety but for family and employees [music] as well. True luxury then shifts again. It is no longer about access to everything. It becomes about moving safely [music] through anything. And the most effective protection often goes unnoticed. [music] In the world of extreme wealth, peace of mind is protected quietly long before [music] danger ever appears. [music] At the beginning of success, attention feels rewarding. Recognition feels earned. Visibility feels like proof that hard work paid off. But as wealth grows, something unexpected happens. Attention slowly becomes exhausting. [music] Strangers recognize faces. Requests multiply. Rumors spread easily. Personal moments stop feeling [music] private. And eventually success starts attracting pressure instead of freedom. This is the stage where priorities begin to change. Luxury stops meaning bigger houses or louder success. Interestingly, the wealthiest families in history are often the ones least visible to the public. Their influence exists quietly. - Germany’s Hidden Billionaires: The Discreet Lives of the Super Rich | Real Life History: Kind: captions Language: en When you picture extreme wealth, you probably imagine vast tech empires and super yachts cutting through the Mediterranean. But look at Germany. It holds more billionaires than any other European nation. Despite numbering over 170 individuals, these mega-wealthy citizens maintain virtually zero public footprint. You're far more likely to see a standard Volkswagen parked outside their homes than a fleet of exotic cars. Financial journalists spend months painstakingly public records just to estimate their assets. For some of the most prominent business families on these rich lists, not a single photograph exists anywhere online. For the German elite, extreme wealth is used to secure the ultimate luxury, a total exit from public life. This quiet fortune rarely comes from sudden tech disruption. It is grounded in the Mittelstand, generations of privately owned medium to large industrial and retail businesses that form the stable base of the country's economy. Take figures like Michael Otto. Over several decades, he transitioned his father's mail-order business into a massive digital enterprise, quietly building a highly profitable global retail empire. Many of these fortunes were built from absolute zero. Following World War II, founders who arrived as destitute refugees slowly rebuilt these massive companies out of the ruins of the country. There are rare new money exceptions who do seek publicity, like fitness chain founder Rainer Schaller. But in the German business ecosystem, highly visible aggressive entrepreneurs are extreme cultural outliers. The true foundation of wealth here is compounding, invisible stability carefully maintained across multiple generations. There are very real physical and psychological reasons for this intense secrecy. Staying out of the public eye lowers the risk of extortion, targeted kidnappings, and home invasions. There is also a heavier historical reality. Some of these inherited fortunes possess ties to the Third Reich, creating an enormous unspoken pressure to avoid any public scrutiny whatsoever. To handle this immense capital, the elite rely on family offices. These highly exclusive asset management firms are tasked entirely with protecting and growing private fortunes that exceed 30 million euros. These offices shield the wealth from internal threats as well. They carefully navigate inheritance disputes, heavy taxation, and the dilution that comes from poor successive management. Even their socializing is isolated. Instead of high-profile galas, multi-million euro deals are negotiated entirely within private VIP football boxes and secluded family-owned hunting lodges. The billionaire lifestyle here is a heavily fortified ecosystem of self-preservation, designed explicitly to keep outsiders looking the other way. While these private portfolios have exploded since the financial crisis, the average middle-class worker in Germany has swallowed losses and watched their wages stagnate. This chart highlights the sheer scale of the current wealth gap. The top 1% of Germans now control a full 25% of the nation's total assets. And if we track the source of that growth, the trend line curves sharply upward. Favorable zero interest rates and an exploding urban real estate market have quietly doubled the property assets of the elite over the past decade. This scale of employment translates into a unique form of political leverage. Because these families provide jobs for hundreds of thousands of people, their private corporate interests are tied directly to national economic stability, giving them a quiet, undeniable influence at the highest levels of government. To counter demands for redistribution, billionaires highlight their status as the Mittelstand, the fragile, essential foundation of the German economy. By hiding their wealth, the ultra-rich successfully deflect the anger of the working class. In an era defined by massive financial inequality, the ultimate luxury is anonymity.
