- How to start a wine cellar with Rudy von Strasser: Kind: captions Language: en hi friends rudy von strasser here in my wine cellar and i'd like to take a moment to talk to you about aging wines and what a better place to do it first of all it's important to know that as soon as a wine is put into the bottle aging starts and that aging is a series of chemical reactions now in a cold cellar and the reason people keep sellers cool is because those reactions happen slower and in a more controlled situation in a warm environment like on your kitchen counter where it's 85 degrees in texas those changes are going to go a lot faster so if you'd like to age wine if you plan on aging wine it's important to have temperature control also you need to know that bottle size matters if you look at this six liter bottle it's going to age much slower than a magnum or a 750 or even a little 375. so if you have 375s at home drink them sooner than you would drink a bigger bottle i want to talk about what happens in aging and i've drawn a little graph that i want to show you and this graph on the x-axis has time and on the y-axis has concentration and the red line i drew is what i call fruit characteristics and in a white wine fruit characteristics maybe um like in a riesling or an esophageal blanc be melons and peaches and apricots aromas like that in a red wine it may be strawberries brambles those aromas start out very high when a wine is very young over time those aromas start to go away and as the wine gets 15 20 years old there won't be any fruit aromas left or very very subtle fruit aromas the blue line i drew i marked as aged characteristics those start out at zero when a wine is young and over time they'll start to increase and the important thing with this graph is that at any time you drink this wine it's going to have very different characteristics as a young wine it's going to be mainly fruit and very little age characteristics at four or five six years old it'll have some fruit and it'll have some age characteristics um you know in the 10-year period it might have a little bit of both but the fruit is starting to go away and at 15 20 30 years there's not going to be much fruit left and a lot of age and the reason that's important is because the biggest variable in aging is what do you like is the consumer's preferences people who have sellers and drink old wines all the time are used to the characteristics that you get with age and those characteristics in a white wine might be things like butterscotch almonds old apple core in a red wine they may be more like coffee olives tobacco cassis things like that and someone who likes old wines and likes age characteristics is going to like that and not like young wines they're not going to really be very interested in the fruit someone who's used to drinking fruity young wines their whole life if you give them this bottle of chateau of the feed rothschild um 30 35 years old this may get a 98 from robert parker it may be worth a thousand dollars but if you give someone who drinks young red wines their whole life they're not going to like it so the biggest variable of all is what do you like is the consumer preference and that's important to know now what i would suggest if you want to figure out what you like is there's two ways one is to try to find a vertical of wine a vertical would be the same winery the same wine maybe the same vineyard designation three or four or five years in a row or three or four or five different years so if you're able to buy the same wine but in different vintages buy four or five different vintages have your friends over maybe put them in a bag put them in labeled glasses and do a tasting and compare them and see which ones you like see if you like old see how they've changed over time the other way is to buy a case of wine and every year open a bottle and see how those are changing and whether you like them and when you like them when my wife rita and i were married we got a case of bordeaux as a wedding present and we opened a bottle every year on our anniversary and it allowed us to reflect on the development of the wine as well as the development of our marriage anyway i hope this helps a little bit with the mysteries of wine aging and wine is a lot of fun and and have fun experimenting

- Wine Storage: 5 MISTAKES to AVOID When Storing and Ageing Wine: Kind: captions Language: en hi everybody this is agnes from nose sediment and today we're going to talk about five mistakes you should avoid when you're storing your wine not every wine is meant for aging and certainly majority of wines are made for immediate consumption with their bright and lively fruit yet there are some wines wine making regions appellations and even certain grape varieties that have shown great aging potential which means that they not only can hold for several years but also develop tertiary aromas adding towards complexity both on the nose and pellet if just like me you are a wine lover chances are great that you have at least several bottles of wine that you have bought to open later possibly after several years or even decade or more wines that are meant for aging will usually cost premium money so it is very important to know what mistakes to avoid when cellaring your wine you do not want to open a bottle of wine that you have stored for 5 or 10 years just to find out that it is spoiled or undrinkable so no matter what the reason you hold your wine collection and even if it's just a single bottle these are five mistakes you should avoid do not store your wines vertically this makes sense for all still wines under the natural cork especially if you do not have a specific place to hold your wines in storing wine horizontally helps to keep cork moist and therefore elastic sealing the wine and limiting the amount of oxygen that enters into the bottle if the bottle is stored vertically for a long period of time the cork might dry out and lose its properties this will lead to increased oxygen permeability and faster maturation of the wine do not store champagne or any other sparkling wine horizontally aha this one seems tricky the thing is the space between wine and cork known as ouyage is filled with co2 molecules and they keep the cork moist therefore there is no reason for champagne bottle to be stored horizontally because it actually even increases the risk for wine to be spoiled by cork taint or trichloroanesol [Music] do not store wines under the light or bright conditions especially if your wine is bottled in transparent glass i really don't think we talk about this enough because faulty wine damaged by light is more common than we actually think therefore do not store your wines next to a window and turn off that light in your wine fridge and storage units that are oftentimes equipped with bright light just to highlight the best bottles you have wine constantly being under lit conditions can quickly develop off aromas caused by oxidation and volatile sulfur compounds and while we're on the subject do not remove that colored foil from your sparkling wine bottles it is there to protect them do not store your wines at room temperature while opinions differ at what temperature exactly we should be aging our wines it is quite clear that temperatures above 15 degrees celsius might accelerate wine aging and as the temperature goes higher it might even imprint cooked flavors into wine and show increased oxidation if you do not have a specific cooling cabinet or wine fridge it makes sense to store your wine lower to the ground rather than up in the shelves because the warm air tends to go up this is if you do not have floor heating do not store your wines for too long i don't buy my wine for investment 90 of my wine collection is bought directly from winemaker and most of these wines have great story behind them therefore i plan to enjoy these wines at some point i have been emotionally attached to some of the very special or expensive bottles of wine for way too long and when i finally did open them they were past their prime and winemakers do make their wine to be enjoyed to be opened up and drunk and bring laughter and joy into our faces wine past its prime might not do that so i invite you all to not hold back that special bottle of wine for too long so here you go these are five mistakes you should avoid when you're storing and aging your wine if however you open bottle of wine and it seems off and you do not know why is that you should watch my other video on six most common wine falls [Applause]

- How Yellow Tail Wine Dominated! - A Case Study for Entrepreneurs: Kind: captions Language: en Hello everybody, Tom Ellsworth here and welcome to case studies with the Biz Doc. This week we're going to talk about Yellow Tail wine. You know, when you think about wine, you think about California, and maybe you think about France. And then people that know their wine really well may know other areas of the world like Spain and Italy and even Argentina that have some great wines. But the typical American and the typical person probably doesn't think about those. They just want to have a great bottle of wine from time to time with friends. And it was an Australian company called the Yellow Tail that brought their brand to the United States and blew the lid off the market. I'm going to show you a couple of things today when we talk about Yellow Tail. The first is, lesson one will be know your target customer. They did a great job of identifying the target, focusing on the target, and executing. The second thing is focus. Within anything you do, you're going to hear how they refused to step away from their plan and stayed focused on it and it became a hit, because they didn't panic and change anything. And lastly, with all types of marketing, be smart, but trust the numbers and trust the research, and let that be your friend as you move forward. So let's take a look at Yellow Tail. Well, Yellow Tail started in Australia as I said. There was a family, an Italian immigrant family in Australia, called the Casella family. And they owned a large vineyard and they were making bulk grapes and selling them to other people and marketing some wines of their own. And they were doing pretty well, and enjoyed time in Australia and had a really nice market presence and everything. And in 2001, that all changed, when a U.S. importer called W. Deutsch and Sons or the Deutsch family, I guess it was called. They were a half a billion dollar distributor in the United States, meaning they would look around the world for wines they thought that could be distributed and sold in the United States and think about a type of wine or a price point, bring all that together and then they would go out and distribute by selling it to wine stores and locations where you and I go and buy it. They had been doing their research and they said, you know, we've been successful with an entry level French wine that was right around the $20 mark, a variety of things they were doing to plan the market. And they said, you know, there is a space in the U.S. market for a $10 wine that could be an everyday wine, or just a friends and family hanging out on the weekend wine. Not like a birthday or anniversary fine wine for special occasions, but just a wine you could drink just about any time. They did the research on the U.S. market and they discovered the tastes of the U.S. consumer were very specific. They found that people if they were going to buy an inexpensive bottle of wine, they wanted it to have more of a strong fruit flavor. They didn't like certain dryness and other attributes, they really weren't interested in. They were just interested in something that was easy to buy, easy to understand, and more importantly, tasted really nice and was easy to drink. The Deutsch family's knowledge of wines and growers around the world led them to Australia. And they knew in Australia there were vineyards and types of grapes that produced the kind of fruit and the kind of wines that they were looking for for the American consumer. So they reached out to the Australian trade commission of all people, that's right, they called the government. And they said, we're looking to partner with an Australian vineyard so that we could kind of create a brand around a wine, bring it to the United States and we want it to be like this, like this, like this. Australia trade commission says, you know, it's our job to help kind of make these partnerships and we know of somebody. So they were directed to the Casella family. Remember I mentioned them. And they've been making wines for awhile. They got together with the Casella family and the Deutsch distributor from America, the Casellas, which were the Italian-Americans running an incredible vineyard in Australia, they got together and they made a partnership, a joint venture to bring a wine to the U.S. And the Casellas came up with the Yellow Tail brand, which the wallaby and all the coloring and everything you see here. They did the branding and marketing in line with what Deutsch had already been talking about the consumer. It was a great marriage that worked out very, very well. They did some very clever and innovative marketing, and they brought it to the United States. You know, one of the things they did in their marketing was very, I don't know if you want to call it luck, because it really wasn't luck. There's a lot of research that was involved. But it was really, they hit it out of the park on the first try. And that was, they had some specific things that they wanted to do by eliminating the complexity of buying wine. And eliminating kind of the snootiness and the foo-foo-ness of is that a good brand, is that a good brand. They wanted to make it very, very easy for people to understand. And so they put together what they called no jargon. So, no wine terminology, nothing complex. Just very simple, straight-forward stuff that a person coming to shop for it could easily understand and pick up a bottle of wine for friends and family and basic daily sipping. It was really, I thought, very, very clever. So clever, in fact, that in their first year, they really took off. The Deutsch family and all the distribution they were doing in the U.S., they had inroads with a lot of people, including a very large grocery chain, discount grocery chain, called Costco. And the Deutsch family cut a deal with Costco, which really led to the explosion of it. Well, I'm going to talk about two things now. First of all, I'm going to talk about what happened in terms of the volume, and then I'm going to get to the marketing a little bit. So, let's go look at some charts. Right here [bottom left] when they introduced the one in 2001, they did about 100,000 cases. Wine is measured in how many cases did you sell. But take a look at this. In a few short years, four and a half years to be, all of a sudden they're at eight million cases, and they, believe it or not, in 2006, I believe it was, they outsold all French wines in America. Outsold them. And this is one easy-drinking, well-marketed fantastically branded wine from Australia. So Italian immigrants, operating a vineyard in Australia, made a wine for the American market, with the Deutsch family. You know you're in a global market when you have all those names and nationalities coming together. Nonetheless, they blew the lid off of it and were very successful. The key to the success wasn't just the Deutsch's family being able to distribute it and get it into stores, because that was key. An experienced winery and an experienced distributor working together, they got a lot farther than any startup vineyard would, any startup winery, because one, they both had experience. But they also had a new product, trying to break in to a new lane. And they literally created new value. One of my favorite books, I love reading from time to time, and I re-read it, it's called Blue Ocean. It's a simple book about marketing strategy. You may have heard of it. If you haven't heard of it, go get yourself a copy and read through it, and I think you're really going to pick up some tips for your own marketing, regardless of the company you're So let's take a look at the Blue Ocean model and find out what the Deutsch distributors and the Casella winemakers did that had such a tremendous effect. First of all, the Blue Ocean strategy is based on one simple premise. Let's build a new value in a brand new blue ocean that nobody has sailed on. The opposite is a red ocean, where there's so many competitors, they're like sharks that are attacking each other, and it's a red ocean. And there's no fish, there's just predators attacking each other, and there's probably no profits left because everybody's discounting, trying to get customers. And there's probably not room for brand-new products. So a blue ocean is where you create new value and a new product to have a new success. And people say, well, wait a minute. Wine's been around for hundreds of years. How could this be new? Well, let's go take a look. They created a wine, basically, for the Coca Cola, Budweiser consumer. This wasn't a wine for super high-end people, who knew all their wines and knew all the terminology, and this wine's very dry, and this. . . . and this one's got, hmmm. . . too much tannins, and all these things that wine lovers would know. They built a great wine for the American consumer to enjoy with friends, family, whatever. And it's basically the Budweiser, Coca Cola of wines, but it's wine and it was a great product. The Blue Ocean framework suggests this. What can you do to raise the level of certain factors above what is currently in the industry now? What can you do to reduce certain things? What can you eliminate? And what can you create? Because if you're bring it all together, you might create a blue ocean where no one was sailing before. So let's take a look at what they did with Yellow Tail. First what did they reduce? Well, the first thing they reduced was wine complexity; all the jargon, and everything that went along with that. And oh, that's not a good vineyard, this is a good vineyard. Well, how do you know which vineyard's which? I'm just at Costco, trying to make a decision. They eliminated all that. So, one of the things that was nice, was all the terminology, all the stuff that went with, boom, they eliminated it. And they reduced the complexities of the understanding the consumer needed and they created, in the product, an easy to drink, fruity flavor that matched with the American palate and taste. And what they found is Americans like bold fruit flavors. And you know what's interesting? It also pointed them to something else. They found in their research that this is also why Starbucks had been popular with America, because it was a strong coffee flavor. And people getting a cup of coffee, doggone it, I want a cup of coffee that tastes like coffee. Well, that same attitude is, well, you know what, I want wine that tastes like fruit. Something that's fruity. Not fruit juice, but something that's got a really rich and good, bold flavor, but it has to be easy drinking. And also, create, you know a lot of fun and ease, when you're going out to buy it. So they did all that, and then they eliminated a lot of the other complexities. So basically what they did, they eliminated complexities, they created a wine that was easy to understand and easy to buy, and they created the new position in the market, a wine you could buy at Costco, identified by the label, what it's all about, and go enjoy a quality product, not cheap box wine that you might buy at 7-11, and I'm not picking on 7-11, I'm not picking on cheap box wine. There are people who enjoy that. This was different. This was really set to be a good quality wine And so with the Casellas and great grapes from Australia and great marketing and a distributor in the Deutsches that new what they were doing and getting the signature on a contract from Costco, they blew the lid off the market and suddenly, they were one of not just successful with Yellow Tail's new product, they were one of the leaders in the American wine market. That's what happened. You know, it's really interesting, when I think about this. I think the lessons come back. Number one, no your target customer. They knew who they were going at. This was going to be like a Budweiser, Coca-Cola of wine, but a quality wine product, not a cheap wine that gives you a headache. Second, focus is your friend. Once they focused on the consumer, they focused on finding the right grapes and the right vineyard, which led Deutsch to talk to the Australia trade commission, which led them to find the Casella family. And the marriage was made. And lastly, when it's time for marketing, you know, maybe they were trying, maybe they weren't, but they created a blue ocean for themselves, a new wine product that you didn't have to go to a wine store and buy, and go to a wine store and have to understand all this terminology and all these different, you know, do I have to go to school before I can go to a wine store and actually buy something? No, they made it so it's easy to buy at Costco and then when you bought it you say, hey, this is pretty good. This is a really great wine to sit here this afternoon and have just great wine. When it's time for an anniversary with your wife, but the champagne, buy the good stuff. But when you're just hanging out with your friends and have a glass of wine, go pick up a Yellow Tail. So, that last lesson I think is there was a little luck. And that the Deutsch family distributors and the Casellas found each other, kind of by accident, but at the end of it, I think we can see that between the two of them, this partnership was a success. So as you build your business, I think what you need to understand is, there may be a little luck, but more often than not, there's a lot of intentional nature to building your business, and staying focused on what it is you're doing, sticking with that, be smart about your marketing, and guess what? You may lead a market. So here we go. All right. - Family Offices: How Billionaires Manage Money Without Banks: Kind: captions Language: en You know there are three stages in your relationship with money. Stage one, you worry about money because you don't have enough. Stage two, you stop worrying because you have more than enough. And stage three, you start to worry again because you have so much money it's becoming a problem and managing everything is a full-time job. This video is about the third scenario. Somewhere past the $100 million mark, you're no longer a wealthy person. You're more of an institution. Money is now referred to as capital. That's when the spreadsheets become staff. And in the world of the rich, the staff is called a family office. And this is how they operate. Welcome to Alux, the place where future billionaires come to get inspired. So any person with a basic financial understanding can manage a million dollars. You put some of it into a safe stock fund that grows over time, a bit into bonds that pay you some steady money, some into real estate, so you own a building or land, and keep a little cash in the bank for emergencies. You can essentially do everything from your phone and just live off the money it makes comfortably for the rest of your life. But what happens when you have a billion? Because look, okay, a bit of a billion still means literally hundreds of millions of dollars. At that level, you're earning millions in interest per month. You might own multiple businesses, properties in different countries, art collections, complex equity positions, and legacy obligations. Even your charity work becomes a logistical beast, requiring lawyers and accountants just to give money away. Wealth, when it reaches this scale, behaves more like a corporation than a bank account. Most people only deal with professionals like lawyers or investment managers a handful of times in their life, if ever. Maybe a lawyer for a will, a banker for a mortgage, or an adviser when retirement is near. But for the ultra wealthy, those needs are constant. Legal questions come up weekly. Big financial decisions happen daily. Managing assets, taxes, investments, and even reputations requires a full-time team. So instead of hiring people one by one for every problem, it's just easier to bring them all in house. You hire your own lawyer, your own banker, your own tax strategist, put them on a payroll, and make their full-time job managing your wealth. That's essentially what a family office is. If you are Batman, the family office is Alfred. And you know, these are the kinds of insights not everybody gets access to, but you'll get the inside scoop on all of this and more within the Alux app. It's not cheap because we've literally paid millions of dollars to the smartest people in the world. And you can get access to all of it for only $1.99 a year. Hundreds of thousands of CEOs, founders, creators, business owners, and anyone with the entrepreneurial grit to make something of themselves are just proof of how valuable this app is. So go to alux.app right now and download it. After you've got it on your phone, since you're a subscriber to our YouTube channel, scan this QR code on screen right now and you'll get 25% off the yearly plan as a thank you from us to you. But let's get back to today's discussion, shall we? Okay, so there are more family offices now than ever before, and they're managing trillions of dollars. The rise of tech billionaires and crypto millionaires created a new wave of first generation wealth and with it a need for customized infrastructure. Traditional banks weren't built for 29year-olds with $400 million practically overnight. That's where family offices came in, offering speed, discretion, and tailored control. As of 2023, estimates suggest there are over 12,000 family offices globally with more than 7,000 based in the US alone. That number has doubled in the last decade. According to research by UBS and Campton Wealth, the average family office manages around $1.2 billion in assets, but some like the Walton families or Bezos's oversee tens of billions. Now, collectively, family offices are estimated to control between 6 and 10 trillion in global wealth. And you know, a massive generational wealth transfer is underway. Over the next 20 years, more than $84 trillion will pass from baby boomers to Gen X and millennials. And that money needs managing. At that level, private banks and wealth managers just aren't enough anymore. You need a full team tailored to you, working only for you. This surge in demand has even created a family office arms race with ultra-wealthy individuals competing to hire the best talent, poaching lawyers, investment analysts, even PR experts from top firms to build up their own private teams. And because they handle everything in-house, family offices develop their own proprietary investment data and insights, giving them an edge over even elite hedge funds or traditional adviserss. So, why exactly are these family offices so fought after? Well, when you hit the $100 million plus mark, your financial life gets exponentially more complex. Not linearly. We're not talking about moving from one to two homes. We're talking about 30 plus legal entities across multiple countries, five plus asset classes like stocks, private equity, real estate, etc. three plus generations with inheritance plans, personal assets like yachts, jets, you name it, as well as staff from security assistants, pilots, the list goes on. Now, imagine trying to pay taxes in three different countries, track ROI across dozens of investment vehicles, plan for inheritance for three kids without them fighting, shielding your assets from lawsuits or divorces. I mean, it's a full-time job for multiple people. And according to Hampton Wealth's global family office report, the average family office employs about 14 full-time staff. And that's for millionaires, not even for billionaires. Now, compare that to Bezos Expeditions, Jeff Bezos's personal family office. It reportedly employs around 159 professionals from lawyers and analysts to philanthropic strategists and venture scouts. To put that into perspective, that's more advisers than most kings had running their entire courts. And their sole job is not to run a kingdom, but to manage one man's fortune. In 2019, Jeff Bezos finalized the most expensive divorce in modern history. His ex-wife, McKenzie Scott, walked away with 25% of the Amazon shares the couple jointly held, worth around $36 billion at the time. Now, most people saw the headlines about the size of the payout, right? But what went unnoticed was how clean and frictionless the entire financial transition appeared. There were no drawn out court battles, no messy disclosures, no massive tax events. And that's because the structure was already in place. The family office handled the transition behind the scenes. It coordinated asset transfers, updated trust structures, and managed risk exposure while keeping Bezos's long-term financial blueprint intact. The office, it didn't react to the divorce. It absorbed it. His control over Amazon remained stable. His wealth continued compounding, and most importantly, the legal and tax implications were minimized through pre-arranged strategies crafted years in advance. In high-profile divorces, brand value can definitely take a hit. But Bezos's public image and Amazon's market confidence barely even wobbled. And that's coordinated behindthecenes damage control executed by a family office with a crisis protocol. So after saying all of that, when should you consider a family office? Well, let's just say you sold your tech company. After taxes, you walk away with half a billion dollars. Congratulations, my friend. You are rich. But now the real work begins. First, your money lands in a private bank. Once you've got more than $1 million in investable assets, the system officially tags you as a high- netw worth individual, and that label upgrades your banking experience. No more call centers. You're now assigned a relationship manager, and you get access to private banking divisions. It's a VIP treatment. But in the wealth world, you're still at the entry level. Cross 30 million in assets and you become ultra high net worth. Now, along with the title, that sounds kind of like a Dragon Ball Z power level, you unlock access to more elite financial tools, hedge funds, private equity, and co-investment opportunities. Banks might even offer global family office services, pre-built teams that manage multiple wealthy clients. It's not a personal family office yet, but it's a preview of what's to come. But only once you pass the $100 million mark do you enter a different league. Now, some banks assign full-time white glove teams whose only job is to serve you. But by then, you might be asking a different kind of question altogether. Why am I still using the bank's people when I could just hire my own? The bank exists to make a profit. So, their advice will always carry some kind of bias. Your lawyer says you need an estate plan. Your accountant warns you about exposure to different tax jurisdictions. Your spouse wants to buy a vineyard in Italy. Each problem spawns five more. You're dealing with private bankers, fund managers, lawyers, and nobody's talking to each other. It's a bit chaotic, right? And that is when a family office becomes a viable option, a good choice. And this is how you set it up. So, you form a limited liability company and you name it after your favorite mountain. Naming it after a favorite mountain or childhood street might sound kind of poetic, but it also makes it pretty hard to Google. You base it in a place that offers favorable tax laws and privacy. Some go with Delaware in the US for its flexible corporate laws and anonymity. Others prefer Singapore, Switzerland, or Dubai. places known for financial privacy, tax efficiency, and investor-friendly regulation. And this becomes your family office. It's just the headquarters. The team might be remote. Then you start to hire. And usually the order is as follows. Money first, then safety, then growth, then logistics. Your first hire is almost always someone who used to manage wealth like yours, but for somebody else, usually a former private banker from a top institution. You offer them better pay and total control. They track every dollar, reduce exposure, and keep you from making expensive mistakes. They consolidate your balance sheet, cash, stocks, real estate, startup equity, art, crypto, yachts, all of it. This person becomes your chief financial officer. Next, the CFO brings in a lawyer, usually specialized in generational wealth. You want someone who eats up trust laws for breakfast. The kind who can set up multigenerational vehicles that survive lawsuits, divorces, or unexpected deaths. They draft your trust documents, create additional holding entities, and firewall your assets. Then comes the tax specialist, ideally a former IRS insider or someone who worked for the big four. They know the loopholes, the treaties, and the lines you shouldn't cross. They design a tax map that minimizes your burden without ever triggering the wrong kind of attention. At this point, your money is secured, but it's not growing. So, you bring in someone with deep access to private deals, hedge funds, and off-market real estate, a chief investment officer. This person will oversee all investments from private equity and hedge funds to real estate and venture capital. And lastly, you hire for logistics. You might need a family office manager, a philanthropy director, or a lifestyle concierge. In Silicon Valley, some family offices hire life coaches or mindfulness consultants for their clients children to help them grow up balanced in a world of access. The Gates family office has a dedicated philanthropy infrastructure. Every dollar gifted is a part of a long-term impact strategy. Some hire former diplomats to help with international travel or relocation planning. Others bring in art curators to manage collections worth millions. Private equity, hedge funds, offshore banking, trusts, they all orbit around one thing, the family office. This is the final control tower of generational wealth. These are the foundational financial systems the rich use to preserve and secure their wealth. The somewhat easy ones to understand. There are a lot more. Okay? Some create private insurance companies and they insure themselves through themselves and keep the unused premiums as profit. Some create think tanks to shape laws in their favor by blocking taxes and regulations. And maybe we'll talk about those later on. Until then, thanks for watching, my friend. We'll see you back here next time. Take care.

- How the World’s Wealthiest People Travel: Kind: captions Language: en There are roughly 295,450 people with at least $30 million. Such individuals, referred to as ultra-high net worth, represent about 0.0037% of the human population, yet they hold about a third, $35 trillion, of the world’s wealth. Therefore, it’s no wonder why a massive, global industry exists, fully centered on supporting the nomadic lifestyles of the world’s wealthiest 295 thousand. As with any socioeconomic class, ultra high net worth individuals are more likely to socialize with other ultra high net worth individuals, yet intriguingly, the number of people earning and spending similarly to you goes down as your income goes up. Therefore, once you get to the top of the income scale, the scarcity of people like you stretches your social networks out to a global scale. Through the years, this led to the creation of the informal but remarkably systematized so-called “Billionaire’s Social Calendar.” The year often starts on St Barts—the traditional New Year’s Eve spot for the wealthiest few—then February might include the Superbowl, and March skiing in Aspen or St Moritz. Spring starts with the Masters Golf Tournament in Augusta, Georgia, while May includes the Cannes Film Festival and the conveniently close Monaco Grand Prix. June’s highlight is the Henley Royal Rowing Regatta in England, July’s is Wimbledon, August’s is the Venice Film Festival, while September often includes a return to Monaco for its yacht show. October’s the Frieze London Art Fair, November might include a trip down under for the Melbourne Cup, and finally, the last major event of the year, before St Barts, of course, is Art Basel Miami Beach. These events are hardly about the events themselves—most wealthy attendees to Monaco’s grand prix hardly care about the racing and its yacht show is far more than a sales event for the vessels—but rather they serve as an exclusive space made available only to wealthy people who want to socialize with other wealthy people. While few go to all, and each adapts the calendar to their preferences, ultra-wealthy individuals have often expressed a feeling of obligation to join the circuit in order to maintain their social standing. Such pressure may have motivated Jeff Bezos, for example, to start his 2022 as he did— on a yacht off St Barts with his partner, Lauren Sanchez. Thanks to publicly-available aircraft transponder data, however, we’re able to see what he did with the rest of his January. Bezos’ pair of G650 jets are too large for St Barts’ short runway, so they waited on neighboring Saint Martin while the billionaire rang in the New Year. Then, on the 1st, N271DV took off for the seven-hour flight to Los Angeles to drop Sanchez off, while the other, N758PB, flew the next day to take Bezos directly home to Seattle. Over the next two weeks, both jets regularly shuttled up and down the west coast between Seattle and Burbank, presumably to take Bezos and Sanchez to visit each other. On January 13th, however, one of the jets flew from Burbank to Maui while the other flew from Seattle and stopped briefly at LAX before continuing on to Maui, presumably carrying Sanchez and Bezos respectively to the billionaire’s new $78 million vacation home on the island, then each jet returned to the mainland four days later. Bezos appeared to visit Sanchez again on the 23rd, then four days later flew directly to Washington DC—Bezos owns the biggest home in the District; the largest newspaper in the city; and his company, Amazon, is constructing its second headquarters across the river in Crystal City, Virginia—so he was likely there for business. But finally, Bezos finished out the month by flying back to Burbank, once again—and he did all of this while threading the needle between highlights on the Billionaire’s social calendar, as just 11 days later he flew down to LA for the Superbowl.&nbsp;&nbsp; Across January, Bezos’ lifestyle called for his G650s to fly 29,000 miles or 46,000 kilometers in total, which is roughly equivalent to flying between Seattle and Sydney four times. To fuel this extent of travel, his jets used about 24,838 gallons or 94,022 liters of fuel, which could otherwise be used to propel the largest passenger aircraft in the world, an A380, across the Atlantic from DC to London with 500 passengers aboard.&nbsp; But Bezos’ travel is indicative of the way that market analysts think ultra-luxury travel is heading. You see, two phenomena are crashing head on into each other to throw the industry into turmoil. First, more people are getting wealthy than ever, as money is becoming increasingly consolidated at the top, and second, merely wealthy people are traveling less frequently than in years past, but spending more than ever. Data indicate that prior to COVID, Americans spent an average of $4,871 on a trip to Greece, but now, it’s all the way up to $6,561. Airline executives have also jubilantly noted that first and business class revenue is remarkably strong, despite the continued weakness of the traditionally lucrative business-travel segment, mostly thanks to leisure travelers opting to pay the premium for more luxurious travel at a greater rate than ever. In New York City, the ultra-luxury hotel market is said to be booming, with all-time high prices and occupancy rates and new super-premium properties opening faster than ever. Summer 2022 saw the opening of the highly-anticipated Aman New York, with the cheapest room starting at $2,600 a night, but more regularly pricing in the three or four thousands. Five years ago, the Aman’s sister property—the Utah desert resort known as Amangiri—started at $1,400 a night, but these days, that’s doubled to $2,800 a night, and even with dynamic pricing charging a hefty premium for the most attractive dates, plenty of nights are regularly sold out, even many months in advance. And of course, for those lucky enough to get a booking at resorts like the Amangiri, they need a way to get there so it’s no wonder that 2021 saw an all-time record-high number of private jet flights—3.3 million globally. This tremendous growth was in large part enabled by the propagation of the on-demand charter business model. Companies like Wheels Up, NetJets, and XO have revolutionized the industry by simplifying one-off charters of private jets, which makes the means of travel far more achievable for the twice-a-year wealthy leisure traveler. These private jets are serviced by fixed-based-operators, which essentially act as private terminals-for-hire, and these are also observing record traffic, leading to more FBOs being developed, which is pushing up prices for airport real-estate and hangars to an all-time-high as well. So, as the industry has grown as large as ever, it’s simultaneously more expensive than ever and more frustrating than ever to get access to a private jet. Essentially, across the ultra-luxury travel segment, there’s a capacity crunch. More people than ever are willing to pay more for a night in a top hotel than most do for a month of rent; or more for a two-hour private flight than most pay for their cars. The pace of industry growth is outmatched by the growth in demand for the segment. The incredible consequence is that the super wealthy have pushed the ultra-wealthy out from their traditional stomping grounds. There simply are not publicly-available travel experiences expensive and exclusive enough for the wealthiest of the wealthiest anymore, so they’re essentially turning to private everything. Yachts fulfill this need well—they allow the ultra-rich to travel on their own terms, bringing the quality of accommodations they expect to wherever they want to go, largely regardless of outside demand. But where they want to go is still largely dictated by that Billionaire’s calendar, so each fall, as the temperatures in the Med drop and the final summer events finish up, a maritime migration begins. Some of the superyachts push off the dock with pared-down crews to begin the lengthy transatlantic passage under their own power, and while most yachts are capable of taking on most ocean crossings, it’s an expensive, impractical, and dangerous endeavor. That’s why these exist—yacht-carrying ships. These ships work year-round connecting the world’s yachting hotspots, but in the fall and spring, it’s all about the transatlantic migration. So, at the end of the Med season, a deluge of vessels descend on yachting hubs like Genoa, Italy and Palma de Mallorca, Spain to load up on one of these vessels—their owners paying up to $100 thousand for the convenience. Upon arrival in the Caribbean, their owners can once again access their yachts, providing convenient access to winter events like Art Basel Miami and New Year’s on St Barts.&nbsp; As with every other segment of the industry, however, the superyacht industry is growing faster than ever, meaning what was previously a refuge from the constraints of land is finding itself increasingly stuck out at sea. Take, for instance, a berth at the Yacht Club de Monaco. First and foremost, it’ll cost you. During the summer season, a night will cost the shortest vessels $275; but if you’d like to dock the same boat during the Monaco Grand Prix, the price skyrockets to over $16,500 a night. To get one of the few spots that can fit the largest 60 or 70 meter vessels, it’ll cost $73,000 a night, but even then, the clubhouse building blocks the view of the track, so to actually see the race, this fee includes access for two to the building itself.&nbsp; But cost is hardly the issue here. Rather, it’s club membership, which requires that you were first sponsored by a member then approved by the admissions committee. Once in, to secure a spot during the Med Season, berth reservations have to be booked months in advance. But for the only event that parallels the Grand Prix in exclusivity, the yacht show, just getting a berth is only half the battle. When showing your superyacht off to other attendees, the best spot to dock is here, across the way, so onlookers can take in the full profile of the vessel rather than just its rear. But getting these spots requires first that the yacht is too long to fit in a traditional spot, and that you, or your captain, has a strong relationship with the event directors—relationships that take years to form.&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; Navigating these busy waters is an expensive pain. Superyacht ownership in general is an expensive Yachts don't actually get someone from one place to the next efficiently at all—on the open ocean they’re generally unpleasantly bumpy rides—and yearly costs generally average around 10% of the boat’s value. Yet, across the board, no matter the length, sales only continue to rise. Anecdotally, the deals cut on the decks of these behemoth boats, and the valuable relationships curated at yacht club bars are said to make the investment worth it. As one new yacht owner told the New Yorker “one deal secured on board will pay it all back many times over.”So, increasingly, the yacht is now perceived as a necessary component of the Billionaire’s social calendar, meaning the same way the wealthiest few are feeling about private aviation and ultra-luxury hotels now extends to the yachting world as well. In response, the ultrawealthy are getting more creative. Over the past five years, just as overall sales and superyacht builds have risen, the number of superyachts over 40 meters being refitted has nearly doubled. More often than not, refitting means a visit to a boat yard for minor alterations. Sometimes, though, these refits are major, which reflects a growing trend: the rise of the expedition yacht. With traditional waters busier than ever and the experiential economy as big as ever, billionaires are rebuilding and buying superyachts capable of taking on more adventurous trips, be it sojourns into the Arctic or lengthy, remote prowls across the Pacific. At the extreme end of this trend is a former Soviet icebreaker now rebuilt as an equally tough and luxurious expedition yacht called Legend. On the other, experiential yachting firms have popped up that create World War II battle simulations in the Pacific for bored kids or set up temporary restaurants on sandbars in the middle of the Maldives.&nbsp; The trend toward exploration and expedition isn’t just a superyacht thing either. At the same time that increasingly numerous millionaires are pushing the ultra wealthy away from their old stops, the interest in and social value of unique experiences is pulling billionaires to services offering one-of-a-kind adventures—from up-close gorilla safaris in Rwanda, to luxurious overland treks to the south pole. These luxpeditions, as they’re called, are individually curated by companies like Brown and Hudson to push their guests through Bear Grylls-like experiences in frigid winter conditions before they escape to tents with heated floors and Michelin star-worthy food waiting on them. Their goal is that the trip, in their words, is seared into the participant’s memory. And if the extreme outdoors isn’t of interest, Brown and Hudson offer the opportunity to travel like James Bond, to live a few days like someone else entirely, or go off on a globe-trotting Grand Tour. For the ultra-wealthy, the options are really endless. As long as it’s legal, said one representative of a referral-only travel service, they’ll do anything for a client. Bespoke traveling for the billionaire class is booming, and as long as the traveler gets a good story out of the experience or the ability to brag about visiting the Salar Uyani salt desert in Bolivia or the most rural reaches of Sudan before any of their friends, they’ll keep paying well into the hundreds of thousands for it. But of course, the true top of the market is what we don’t know about yet. Increasingly, wealthy people are traveling to be seen—either in person or on social media. In response, ultra wealthy people are increasingly traveling to disappear—as reflected in the record sales of secluded $30, $40, and $50 million vacation homes in places like Hawaii, Colorado, and the Caribbean. We don’t have a great sense of the ins and outs of the most expensive travel experiences, because the secrecy is part of the premium itself. By the time we do, the top of the wealth pyramid will have moved onto something else—always staying one step ahead of the merely wealthy.&nbsp; If you want to learn more about the yachting world, and specifically how builders are managing to design ever larger and more luxurious yachts, I’d recommend checking out this episode of the show Ultimate Processes on Curiosity Stream. Tens of thousands of Wendover viewers are already subscribed to CuriosityStream, but for those of you who aren’t yet, the good news is that it’s actually a really good deal when signing up through our link, because that also gets you access to Nebula for free. Nebula is a streaming site founded by myself and a bunch of the other creators that Wendover viewers likely already watch, and as a creator owned and operated platform, it was designed from the ground up to be the best home to the stuff we make. For example, by losing the ad-supported model, we’re able to make videos on more niche subjects that we know would appeal to our core audience, but potentially not to the size of audience that would make it profitable on YouTube. We’re also able to make higher-budget stuff, called Nebula Originals—we at Wendover have made all these, each of which is some of our best stuff. You’d probably be blown away by the price if it was just for that, but it’s not, because the cheapest way to get access to Nebula, as I mentioned, is through CuriosityStream. When you all go to CuriosityStream.com/Wendover and sign up for any of their subscriptions, they’ll throw in a Nebula subscription completely for free. - They Said She Was Just the Assistant—Now She Runs the Entire Company: Kind: captions Language: en The scent of polished mahogany and old money clung to the executive floor of Richardson Global. Victoria Richardson, VP of operations, Harvard MBA, and the silent architect behind 5 years of record-breaking growth stood in her corner office as her father's words echoed off the glass walls. Clean out your desk by noon. He didn't even flinch. dressed in a tailored navy suit and a Hermes tie Victoria had given him last Christmas. Her father looked more like a politician than a CEO. His voice carried the same patronizing calm he used when she was 12 and dared to suggest a new idea at the dinner table. The board agrees. You're not cut out for this. Behind him, the skyline loomed. Victoria's name was still on the frosted glass of the door. For now, he continued, the family legacy needs stronger hands. Michael will take over. Michael, of course, her older brother, the golden boy with a string of botched projects behind him and a talent for spin. The same Michael who nearly tanked the Tokyo division but somehow failed upward into yet another executive title. Victoria nodded, lips pressed into a calm smile. Understood. She turned back to her desk, sorting through the evidence of five years of sweat and sacrifice sustainability initiatives, tech integration plans, emerging market strategy proposals, all dismissed because they weren't how we've always done things. She carefully slid personal items into a box, a silver photo frame of her and her late grandmother, a ceramic tea mug, and her minimal decor. The real assets, the documents, the research, the intel were already locked away in her encrypted server. Her phone buzzed. Ellen illegal. Documents ready. Operation Phoenix green lit. Waiting on your call. Victoria typed only two words in reply. Proceed now. Across the room, her father hesitated, thrown off by her lack of resistance. He'd expected tears or some emotional plea. That's what they all expected from her. A daddy's girl who couldn't survive outside the protective umbrella of the family empire. He added, "The severance package is generous enough to start something small." She smiled. "That's thoughtful. I'll consider it." He had no idea. Phoenix Capital, the boutique consultancy she had launched 2 years ago, was now a ghost network of shell companies and investor pools that had been steadily acquiring Richardson Global Stock under Anonymous Holdings, she walked past him. "Good luck with the Anderson merger," she added casually, his head jerked up. How do you know about just a guess? She didn't mention the months she'd spent documenting Anderson's price fixing practices, offshore accounts, and backdoor deals enough to sink the entire deal in one leak to the SEC, or that she'd already met with Anderson's CEO last week. Her phone buzzed again. Asian markets closed, final batch secured, Europe opens in 3 hours. As she stepped into the elevator, she took one last look at the office her father thought he'd stripped away from her. What he didn't know was that she wasn't leaving empty-handed. She was burning the foundation and rising from it. The heavy doors of Richardson Global closed behind Victoria with a finality that felt less like exile and more like liberation. Outside, Manhattan pulsed as always, horns blared, people rushed, taxis surged, but Victoria walked with deliberate calm. She wasn't defeated. She was detonating a plan years in the making. Savannah allowed herself a small smile, had held high, not because the journey was over, but because she had navigated its shadows with grace and unwavering resolve. Tonight, the silence that had once swallowed her voice finally broke, and it had echoed louder than any accusation ever could. The days following the gala unfolded with a shifting tide of consequences. The socialite, whose calculated sabotage had been caught in the unforgiving light of truth, faced swift and decisive action. The Arts Foundation's board convened promptly, uncomfortable, but resolute in their duty to uphold integrity. Public announcements declared her removal from the foundation's membership and invitations revoked from all affiliated events and organizations. Her name circulated briefly in social columns, but now tinged with scandal rather than praise. Invitations she once accepted with a smile were returned unopened, and whispers of her disgrace rippled through elite circles like sudden storm clouds. The veneer of glamour cracked for those who had once envied her effortless presence. Savannah Lee, by contrast, found herself stepping into a new light. The gala's head organizer, Evelyn Pierce, not only offered a formal public apology, but extended an unexpected and generous gesture, a promotion to assistant event coordinator, complete with a modest raise and the opportunity to shape future events rather than simply serve them. Evelyn's voice was steady and sincere as she conveyed the offer, acknowledging Savannah's professionalism, eyesight for detail, and the courage it took to stand against falsehood. You deserved better from the start. Evelyn said, "We want you with us, not in the shadows, but leading with us." For Savannah, it was more than a job offer, it was a restoration of dignity and trust. She weighed the gesture thoughtfully. Her independence was hard one, and while she appreciated the invitation, her days of silent compliance were over. Yet the path forward gleamed fuller now, lit by recognition and respect she had never dared to expect. The event manager, Marcus Gillespie, who had dismissed Savannah without a second thought, approached her at the Foundation headquarters with a cautious expression. His usual veneer of confidence was cracked by discomfort as he coughed and attempted to smooth over prior mistakes. Savannah," he began, voice hesitant. "I want to say, "I'm sorry for how things were handled. You were wronged." The sincerity in his tone felt unfamiliar, almost awkward. She listened without immediate response, eyes steady. "Thank you, Marcus. I appreciate that. But I've learned that respect isn't given lightly. It's earned by actions, not just words. Her words were calm, but firm, showing the inner strength that had been tempered by experience. Walking out of the office later that day, Savannah felt a quiet pride she hadn't known in years. The city outside shimmerred with evening lights, the hum of life around her softened by the steady beat of her own heart. She no longer walked unnoticed or underestimated. Her head held high, Savannah moved forward with clear purpose. She was not only cleared of blame, but united with allies who valued her integrity and skills. The silent, modest woman they had once judged had become a figure of respect. Welcomed not as an outsider, but as a vital part of a world she'd quietly worked to be part of all along. That night, as Savannah left the impressive building behind her, the crisp air was filled with distant laughter and music from celebrations still unfolding. She inhaled deeply, feeling the weight of past burdens lifting, replaced by the promise of a future shaped by her own determination. Her steps echoed confidently, each one a quiet defiance against the shadows of doubt she had once carried. At last, Savannah Lee was no longer invisible.

- His Assistant Splashed Wine on Me Before 300 Guests: “You’re Not Worthy of Him.”I Calmly Called My …: Kind: captions Language: en At the engagement banquet, my fiance's assistant splashed red wine on me in front of 300 guests. A country bumpkin like you is worthy of marrying Junyi. I declare this engagement canceled. I calmly wiped the wine stains from my face and made a phone call. Dad, did you hear that? Some say I'm not worthy. In the banquet hall of a five-star hotel, the crystal chandeliers sparkle brilliantly. Today is my engagement party with Song Junyi, attended by over 300 guests. All important clients and partners from both companies. I sit quietly in a corner wearing a simple white dress, playing with my phone. The engagement was entirely the idea of our parents. Song Junyi is a well-known architectural designer in Haicheng, and his Junyi Design has a great reputation in the industry. As for me, in his eyes, I'm just an ordinary employee at an investment company. We've known each other for 3 years and have been in a relationship for two, but the number of times we've actually gone on a date alone can be counted on one hand. Every time I ask him out, he always says he's busy with work later. I found out that every time he rejected me, he was with his assistant, Bai Manxue. Jiang Yao, why are you sitting here alone? Song Junyi walked over, wearing a perfect social smile on his face. He was wearing a well-tailored black suit today, looking mature and steady. Waiting for you. I looked up at him. Didn't you say you were going to introduce me to your friends? Oh, right. He adjusted his tie somewhat awkwardly. We'll talk about it later. I need to go greet the other guests first. After saying that, he hurried away. I continued to look down at my phone, but out of the corner of my eye, I saw him walk toward Bai Manxue on the other side of the venue. Bai Manxue was wearing a red evening gown today, with exquisite makeup, radiant and dazzling. She stood next to Song Junyi, looking like a perfectly matched couple. As for me, I felt more like an outsider. Miss Jiang, hello. A middle-aged man walked up to me, dressed in a sharp suit with a respectful expression. I am Liming, the CFO of Junyi Design. President Song asked me to accompany you for a chat. I nodded politely. Hello, Director Li. Where does Ms. Jiang work? Liming sat beside me, trying to make conversation. Just an ordinary investment company, doing some fund management. I replied casually. A flicker of disdain flashed in Liming's eyes, but he quickly concealed it. I see. The investment industry must be quite competitive, right? Not bad. The atmosphere became a bit awkward. Liming clearly had little interest in me, a regular employee. And after a few perfunctory words, he made an excuse and left. I picked up my phone again, about to continue playing the game, when suddenly I heard a burst of mocking laughter not far away. I heard Mr. Song's fiance is just a clerk at an investment company. Yeah, and she's not even that good-looking. She's completely unworthy of Mr. Song. Mr. Song is so outstanding. He could have any woman he wants. Why does he have to follow his family's arrangements? I bet this marriage won't last long before they get divorced. I looked up and saw several elegantly dressed women whispering at a table not far away. They thought I couldn't hear them, but at this distance, every word was crystal clear. I ignored them and continued scrolling through my phone. In situations like this, gossip like this is something I've long grown used to. Distinguished guests, dear friends. Song Junyi suddenly walked onto the stage and picked up the microphone. Thank you all for coming to the engagement banquet for Jiang Yao and me today. On this special day, I would like to say a few words to my fiance. His gaze fell upon me, yet there was not a trace of warmth in his eyes. Jiang Yao, although our marriage was arranged by our families, I hope we can support each other and grow together. The words were formal, very polite. Scattered applause echoed from below the stage. I also politely applauded. At that moment, Bai Manxue suddenly walked up from below the stage. She held a microphone in her hand, with a sweet smile on her face. Excuse me, I'd like to say a few words. Song Junyi was taken aback for a moment, but did not stop her. Bai Manxue cleared her throat and looked at me in the audience. Jiang Yao, I know you might not be very familiar with Junyi's work. As his assistant, I'd like to remind you, Junyi is a workaholic who often works late into the night, so you need to be more understanding of him. Also, he doesn't like overly clingy women, so you should give him enough space. Most importantly, his career is just getting started, and he needs a woman who can support him, not hold him back. Her words sounded like well-intentioned advice, but each sentence carried a sharp implication. The guests below the stage began to whisper among themselves. This assistant makes a good point. Jiang Yao really doesn't seem to suit President Song. What help could a small employee possibly offer President Song? I slowly stood up and walked to the front of the stage. Everyone's eyes were fixed on me. Bai Manxue, seeing me step onto the stage, flashed a hint of satisfaction in her eyes. Assistant Bai is absolutely right. I took the microphone. My voice calm. I truly don't know much about Junyi's work. However, I'd like to ask, as an assistant, aren't you overstepping your authority? Bai Manxue's expression changed slightly. I was just kindly reminding you. Kindly? I laughed. Then let me kindly remind you as well. As Junyi's fiance, I hope you can clearly understand your own position. Don't do anything that could be misunderstood. Bai Manxue's face instantly flushed red. What do you mean by that? You know exactly what I mean. The atmosphere in the audience began to grow tense. Song Junyi quickly walked over, wanting to ease the awkwardness. All right, today is the engagement banquet. Let's all be a little happier. Bai Manxue suddenly grabbed the red wine glass on the table, which was half filled with red wine. Jiang Yao, what gives you the right to say that about me? I worked for Junyi for 3 years, working overtime until late at night every day. Do you know how much I've given? And you, besides being a fiance, what else do you have? Her voice grew sharper, and her emotions became more intense. You're just a small employee earning a few thousand a month. What right do you have to boss me around here? After saying that, she suddenly splashed the red wine at me. Splash. The red wine spilled on my white dress, instantly staining it a dark red. The venue fell into a dead silence. Everyone held their breath. Red wine dripped down my cheeks, and the dark red stains on my white dress looked particularly glaring under the lights. The venue fell into complete silence, with the gaze of over 300 guests fixed on me. Someone took out their phone and started recording. Someone covered their mouth, looking shocked. More people were gleefully waiting to see the drama unfold. Bai Manxue stood on the stage, her chest heaving violently. She was even a bit surprised by her own impulsiveness just now, but soon, her expression grew even more arrogant. Jiang Yao, this is the consequence of challenging me. Do you think you can do whatever you want just because you're a fiance? Let me tell you, this world values strength, not connections. A murmur of whispers rose from the audience. This assistant is right. Jiang Yao is indeed too arrogant. What right does a small employee have to show off here? I slowly took out a handkerchief and wiped the red wine off my face bit by bit. The movements were slow, very deliberate, as if everything that had just happened had nothing to do with me. Song Junyi. I looked toward my fiance in the audience. He stood there, his face pale, his eyes flickering. Your assistant splashed wine on me in front of 300 guests. Don't you have anything to say? Song Junyi opened his mouth, but no sound came out. His gaze shifted between me and Bai Manxue, clearly unsure how to handle the situation. Junyi, say something. Bai Manxue suddenly grabbed his arm. I've worked for you for so many years. How dare she humiliate me like this? Didn't you say that I am your most important partner? Song Junyi winced slightly in pain from her grip, frowning, but he still didn't speak. I smiled. It seems that President Song has already made his stance clear with his silence. I picked up the microphone again, my voice resonating clearly throughout the entire venue. Ladies and gentlemen, I apologize for letting you witness such a scene. However, I believe this is also a good thing. At least it allowed me to see the true colors of some people. People in the audience began whispering to each other. What does Jiang Yao mean by this? Is she going to cause trouble? In such a situation, she should have held back. Bai Manxue saw that I didn't flee in a panic as expected, and instead became even more arrogant. Jiang Yao, stop pretending here. Today's engagement party was a joke from the start. Who do you think you are? What makes you think you can marry Junyi? Is it your meager salary or your utterly insignificant family background? Her words grew increasingly harsh, and the guests below the stage began to murmur among themselves. This assistant has a point. Jiang Yao really isn't worthy of President Song. With such a mismatch in social status, this marriage is bound to run into problems sooner or later. Several executives from Junyi Design also began to chime in. Assistant Bai is right. Miss Jiang, it would be better for you to face reality. Mr. Song is so outstanding. You two really aren't a good match. Breaking up now is best for everyone. Faced with everyone's mockery and doubts, I remained calm. I slowly walked up to Bai Mengxue. My voice soft, but each word clearly audible. Bai Mengxue, are you sure you want to do this? Of course. She straightened her chest. I want everyone to know that you, Jiang Yao, are not worthy of standing here. You are not worthy of marrying Jun Yi. This engagement banquet is a joke. She turned to the guests below the stage, her voice sharp. Everyone, I hereby officially announce, in my capacity as senior assistant of Jun Yi Design, that this engagement is cancelled. Jiang Yao, you can get lost. A commotion erupted in the venue. Someone started to applaud. Someone began to jeer. More people took out their phones to record, ready to spread this good show. Song Jun Yi finally spoke up. Mengxue, don't be like this. But his words were feeble, more like a mere formality. Bai Mengxue smiled triumphantly. Jun Yi, you finally admit it. We've been in love for 3 years. Why should we separate because of a family arrangement? We should be brave and stay together. After saying this, she suddenly hugged Song Jun Yi. A wave of exclamations erupted from the audience. I see, Director Song and the assistant are the real deal. Jiang Yao had her man snatched away. I watched this scene unfold, feeling completely indifferent inside. Even there's a sense of relief. I took out my phone and dialed a number. Dad, can you hear me? Someone said I'm not worthy. There was a 2-second silence on the other end of the phone, followed by a low voice. I understand. I hung up the phone and picked up the receiver again. Ladies and gentlemen, since Assistant Bai has already announced the cancellation of the engagement on behalf of Jun Yi Design, I have nothing more to say. However, before I leave, I'd like to clarify one thing. I'm not some small-time employee at an investment firm. I am the chairman of the Jiangshi Investment Group, Jiang Yao. The Jiang Yao of Jiangshi Investment. As soon as the words were spoken, the venue instantly erupted into chaos. Jiangshi Investment Group, one of the largest investment companies in Haicheng, with assets exceeding hundreds of billions. And Jiang Yao is that legendary eldest daughter of the Jiang family, never appearing in the media, an enigmatic existence. Jun Yi Song's face instantly turned deathly pale. Mengxue Bai was also stunned, her mouth hanging wide open. The guests below the stage began to whisper among themselves. Impossible. How could Jiang Yao possibly be the chairman of Jiangshi Investment? Hasn't the eldest daughter of the Jiang family never made public appearances? Is this a joke? I ignored their doubts and continued speaking. 3 years ago, when Jun was first established, the funding chain broke and it almost went bankrupt. It was me, in the name of Jiang's Investment, who injected 50 million to save this company. 2 years ago, Jun Yi Design was bidding for the Haicheng Central Business District project and encountered a formidable opponent. It was me who used my connections to make the competitor withdraw voluntarily. 6 months ago, Jun Yi Design wanted to expand its scale, but the bank refused to grant a loan. It was my guarantee that made the bank approve the 200 million loan. With every word I spoke, Song Jun Yi's face grew paler. The guests below the stage also began to believe what I was saying. Because many people present were aware of these matters, they just didn't know that I was the one pulling the strings behind the scenes. The air in the venue seemed to freeze. Bai Mengxue's face turned from red to pale, then from pale to ashen, her lips trembling as she struggled to speak. Song Jun Yi slumped even further into his chair, his hands clenched tightly, his knuckles turning white. I continued, of course, all of that is in the past now. Since Assistant Bai believes I am unworthy, there is no need for me to continue this meaningless relationship. Starting today, Jiangshi Investment will completely withdraw all investments from Jun Yi Design, including the three ongoing projects and the 2 billion loan that is about to mature. Song Jun Yi suddenly stood up, his voice trembling. Jiang Yao, you can't do this. There are still over 80 employees in the company, and they are all innocent. If those projects are halted, it will affect many people's livelihoods. I looked at him, my gaze as calm as still water. You should go talk to your Assistant Bai about these matters. After all, it was she who announced the of your company. Bai Mengxue finally snapped back to reality. Her face as pale as paper. No, that's not how it is. I just I just acted on impulse. Just then, the door to the venue was suddenly pushed open. A middle-aged man in a black suit walked in, followed by over a dozen bodyguards. Seeing him, everyone present gasped in shock. Jiang Chencheng, going against the entire Jiang family's investments. His words were not loud, but each one was filled with intimidation. Shocking, the heiress of Jiangshi Investment gets wine splashed on her by a mistress in public. Jun Yi Design faces bankruptcy crisis. Earthquake in Haicheng's business world. "Time cannot be turned back. Some mistakes, once made, are made. Some hurts, once caused, are caused. I gave you a chance, but you didn't cherish it. Now, it's all too late." "No." My answer was straightforward. "Jiang Yao, I know I have no right to ask for your forgiveness, but Bai Meng Shu, she just made a mistake in a moment of confusion. If we had been together back then because we truly loved each other and not because of our families' arrangements, would the outcome have been different?" Upon hearing these conditions, Song Junyi's expression turned very unpleasant. "Jiang Yao, these terms are too harsh, especially the equity transfer. Junyi Design is my life's work. I can't." "Then there's nothing more to say." I cut him off. "Since you think it's harsh, we'll proceed as planned. The bank will start collecting the loan tomorrow, and it must be fully repaid within 3 days. If it's not paid, the company will be seized." "Jiang Yao, you can't do this." He said anxiously. "You're forcing my hand." "Force you?" I sneered. "Song Junyi, get your facts straight. He paced back and forth in the office, clearly engaged in an intense internal struggle. "Give me some time to think it over." "Okay. Sure." I glanced at my watch. "I'll give you 24 hours. At this time tomorrow, give me your answer. If you don't come, I'll take it as a refusal." Song Junyi nodded and turned to leave the office. After he left, Su Ya walked in.
